docs.title: VICA — AI-Powered Recruitment Screening
docs.text:
On-Premise. Explainable. Open Source.

The first open-source AI recruitment screener with citation-level explainability — built for EU AI Act compliance.

    B --> C["🧠 AI Skill Extraction"]

  • Complete Data Sovereignty

    All data stays on your hardware. No cloud APIs, no third-party processors, no cross-border transfers. GDPR compliant by architecture.

  • Citation-Level Explainability

    Every skill assessment links to verbatim CV quotes. 4-level proficiency, justification scoring, and targeted interview questions.

  • SPEA2 Multi-Objective Ranking

    Pareto-optimal candidate selection across all job skills simultaneously — no information loss from single-score collapse.

  • EU AI Act Ready

    Open-source and auditable. Built-in validation framework (Precision, Recall, F1). Recruiter oversight loop satisfies Art. 13 & 14.

  • Zero License Fees

    Entire stack is open source: FastAPI, React, Ollama, vLLM. 60–85% lower TCO vs cloud alternatives over 5 years.

  • Smart GPU Orchestration

    Single GPU shared between OCR and LLM with automatic switching. No dual-GPU required — works on an RTX 4090.

docs.title: SPEA2 in VICA: Theory and Implementation
Algorithm Flow
docs.text:
graph TD
    A["Candidate × Skill<br/>Binary Matrix"] --> B["Compute Weights<br/>required (2.0) × position factor"]
    B --> C["Build Objective Vectors<br/>objectives[i,j] = weight[j] × binary[i,j]"]
    C --> D["Compute Strength S(i)<br/>count of individuals i dominates"]
    D --> E["Compute Raw Fitness R(i)<br/>sum of S(j) for all j that dominate i"]
    E --> F{"R(i) = 0?"}
    F -->|"Yes"| G["Non-dominated<br/>(Pareto front)"]
    F -->|"No"| H["Dominated<br/>(lower priority)"]
    G --> I["Tie-break by<br/>weighted objective sum"]
    H --> I
    I --> J["Select top archive_size<br/>candidates by lowest R"]

docs.title: What SPEA2 Is
docs.text:
SPEA2 (Strength Pareto Evolutionary Algorithm 2, Zitzler et al.) is a multiobjective evolutionary algorithm. It ranks solutions using Pareto dominance and density so that:

  1. Convergence: Prefer solutions that are not dominated by others (on the Pareto front).
  2. Diversity: Among equally good (e.g. non-dominated) solutions, prefer those in less crowded regions.

In VICA we use it in a one-shot way: no evolution, just one “environmental selection” step to pick the best archive_size candidates given their objective vectors.

docs.title: Objectives in VICA
docs.text:
• Each candidate has a vector of job skills (binary: has skill or not).
  • Each job skill has a weight (required vs preferred, and position in the list).
  • Objective vector for candidate i: objectives[i, j] = weight[j] * (1 if candidate i has job skill j else 0) So we have one objective per job skill, and we maximize all of them (more/better skills = better).

docs.title: 1. Pareto dominance (maximization)
docs.text:
Solution a dominates b (a \succeq b) iff:

  • a_k \geq b_k for all objectives k, and
  • a_k > b_k for at least one k.

In code: dominates(a, b, maximize=True) → np.all(a >= b) and np.any(a > b) ✓ Correct.

docs.title: 2. Strength S(i)
docs.text:
S(i) = number of individuals that i dominates.

S(i) = \bigl| j \mid i \succeq j \bigr|

In code: strength(i, objectives, ...) counts j \neq i such that dominates(objectives[i], objectives[j]). ✓ Correct.

docs.title: 3. Raw fitness R(i)
docs.text:
R(i) = sum of strengths of all individuals that dominate i.

R(i) = \sum_{j \succeq i} S(j)

  • Minimize R: lower is better.
  • R(i) = 0 ⇒ i is non-dominated (nobody dominates it).
  • High R(i) ⇒ i is dominated by many strong solutions.

In code: raw_fitness(i, objectives, strengths, ...) sums strengths[j] over all j that dominate i. ✓ Correct.

docs.title: 4. Density D(i) (SPEA2 full version)
docs.text:
To separate solutions with the same R(i) (e.g. all non-dominated), SPEA2 adds a density term:

  • \sigma^k_i = distance from i to its k-th nearest neighbor in objective space (e.g. Euclidean).
  • k is often \sqrt{\text{population size}} or 1.
  • Density:

D(i) = \frac{1}{\sigma^k_i + 2}

(The +2 keeps the denominator positive and D(i) < 1.)

Then:

F(i) = R(i) + D(i)

Fitness is still minimized: lower F = better. So among same R, less crowded (larger \sigma^k_i) ⇒ smaller D(i) ⇒ better F.

In our code: We do not implement D(i). We use F = R only (no density). So we have a simplified SPEA2: correct dominance, strength, and raw fitness; no k-NN density.

docs.title: 5. Selection
docs.text:
• Standard SPEA2: Keep the N individuals with lowest F(i) = R(i) + D(i) (and use a truncation method to keep boundary solutions).
  • Our implementation: We keep the archive_size individuals with lowest R(i), and tie-break by weighted objective sum (higher sum = better). So among candidates with the same R, we prefer those with more/better skills (higher weighted sum). ✓ Consistent with “best candidates” for a job.

docs.title: Summary: Is the Implementation Theoretically Correct?
docs.text:
Component SPEA2 definition VICA implementation Verdict Dominance a \succeq b (max) dominates(a, b, maximize=True) ✓ Correct Strength S(i) of j that i dominates strength(i, ...) ✓ Correct Raw fitness R(i) \sum_{j \succeq i} S(j) raw_fitness(i, ...) ✓ Correct Density D(i) 1/(\sigma^k_i + 2) Not implemented Omitted Total fitness F = R + D, minimize We use F = R only, minimize R Simplified Tie-break By density / truncation By weighted objective sum (higher = better) Reasonable

So:

  • Theoretic correctness: The parts we implement (dominance, strength, raw fitness, “minimize R”) match SPEA2. The only difference is we omit density and use a tie-break by weighted sum instead of k-NN density.
  • Why this is still reasonable for VICA:
  • One-shot selection (no evolutionary loop).
  • Many candidates can have R=0 (non-dominated); tie-breaking by “how many weighted skills they have” picks the stronger candidates for the job.
  • Adding D(i) would favor diversity in the 11-D skill space; for “top 10 for this job” your current choice (R + weighted sum) is a sensible and consistent simplification.

If you ever want full SPEA2 (e.g. for more diversity in the archive), you would add the k-NN density step and use F(i) = R(i) + D(i) for selection; the current code gives you the right structure to plug that in.

docs.title: VICA Market Analysis — AI-Powered Recruitment Screening
docs.text: Executive document for strategic positioning of VICA in the AI recruitment technology market. All figures in USD. Data as of Q1 2026.

docs.title: 1. Executive Summary
docs.text:
The global AI in recruitment market was valued at approximately \(661 million in 2023** and is projected to reach **\)1.12 billion by 2030, growing at a CAGR of 7.8% (Verified Market Research). This sits within the broader HR technology market, estimated at $35–40 billion in 2024, reflecting an industry-wide acceleration toward intelligent automation across the talent lifecycle.

Today, the market is dominated by cloud-only SaaS platforms — Workday, Eightfold.ai, iCIMS, SAP SuccessFactors, and others — that require organizations to transfer sensitive candidate data to third-party infrastructure. Despite the proliferation of AI-powered screening tools, no major vendor offers a fully on-premise, open-source AI screening solution with citation-level explainability. This gap is not merely a product oversight; it reflects an industry that built its architecture before regulation caught up.

Regulation is now catching up. The EU AI Act (Regulation 2024/1689), effective in phases from 2025 to 2027, explicitly classifies employment AI as "high-risk" — mandating transparency, human oversight, and bias audits for any system used to screen or evaluate job candidates. Simultaneously, GDPR enforcement is intensifying: cumulative fines exceeded EUR 4.5 billion by 2024, with increasing scrutiny on automated profiling and cross-border data transfers. Organizations using cloud-based CV screening are exposed on both fronts.

VICA is architecturally positioned to address this regulatory gap. Candidate data never leaves the organization's premises. The algorithm is open-source and fully auditable. Explainability is built into the core architecture — not bolted on as a compliance afterthought — with verbatim CV citations, 4-level proficiency assessments, justification scoring, and interview question generation for every candidate-skill match. A recruiter validation loop ensures human oversight is structural, not optional.

This analysis examines the competitive landscape, regulatory dynamics, and cost economics that define VICA's market opportunity.

docs.title: 2.1 Market Size and Growth
docs.text:
Segment Size (2023–2024) Projected (2030) CAGR Source AI in Recruitment $661M $1.12B 7.8% Verified Market Research Recruitment Software $3.1–3.4B $5.3–5.7B 7.2–8.3% Grand View Research HR Technology (Total) $35–40B $65–76B 9–11% MarketsandMarkets

The AI recruitment segment, while modest in absolute terms, represents the highest-leverage layer of the broader HR technology stack. Screening and matching intelligence directly determines hiring quality, time-to-fill, and compliance exposure — making it disproportionately valuable relative to its market size.

docs.title: 2.2 Market Segmentation
docs.text:
The recruitment technology market can be segmented into four tiers based on scope, pricing, and AI depth:

Tier 1 — Enterprise HCM Suites: Workday, SAP SuccessFactors, Oracle HCM Cloud. These platforms span the full HR lifecycle — from workforce planning to payroll — with recruiting as one module among many. Annual costs range from $300K to $5M+ depending on organization size and module selection. AI features in recruiting (matching, screening, recommendations) are add-ons to the core platform, not the primary value proposition. These vendors optimize for breadth, not screening depth.

Tier 2 — ATS Platforms: iCIMS, Greenhouse, Lever (Employ Inc.), SmartRecruiters, Bullhorn. These platforms provide structured hiring workflows — job posting, application tracking, interview scheduling, offer management — at $6K to $200K/year. AI capabilities are typically limited to basic resume parsing, keyword matching, and rudimentary scoring. The value proposition is workflow efficiency, not intelligent screening.

Tier 3 — AI-Native Point Solutions: Eightfold.ai, HireVue, Paradox, Beamery, Phenom, Harver/Pymetrics, SeekOut, Textio. These vendors lead with AI as the core product — talent intelligence graphs, video assessment, conversational screening, predictive analytics. Annual costs range from $10K to $500K. These platforms offer the deepest AI capabilities in the market but are universally cloud-only and often operate as black boxes, providing scores or recommendations without transparent reasoning.

Tier 4 — Open-Source / On-Premise: VICA, OpenCATS (legacy PHP-based ATS with no AI capabilities). This segment is virtually unserved. OpenCATS has not had a significant release in years and offers no AI matching, no skill extraction, and no modern architecture. VICA is the first entrant to combine on-premise deployment with AI-powered screening at a level competitive with Tier 3 vendors.

docs.title: 2.3 Growth Drivers
docs.text:
• Skills-based hiring replacing credential-based hiring — LinkedIn reports 40% year-over-year growth in skills-first job postings, reflecting a structural shift away from degree requirements toward demonstrated capabilities
  • Labor shortages forcing automation — the average corporate job opening receives approximately 250 applications, making manual screening economically unsustainable
  • Remote work expanding candidate pools globally — organizations now receive applications across jurisdictions, compounding both volume and data residency complexity
  • DEI mandates driving demand for bias-reduction AI — structured, auditable screening reduces subjective bias in candidate evaluation
  • AI regulation creating compliance requirements — the EU AI Act, NYC Local Law 144, and Colorado AI Act are creating a new category of mandatory compliance spend for employment AI
  • Internal mobility and talent marketplace platforms gaining traction — organizations increasingly apply matching intelligence to internal candidates, not just external applicants

Enterprise HCM suites (Workday, SAP, Oracle) bundle recruiting within broader HR platforms. VICA does not compete at this level — it targets the screening and matching intelligence layer specifically, where these suites are weakest.

VICA excels in the AI screening and matching layer — the core intelligence that determines which candidates advance and why. This is the highest-leverage stage of the recruitment funnel, where poor decisions are most costly and bias risk is greatest.

VICA is designed to complement, not replace, an existing ATS — it slots into the screening stage as an AI intelligence module, feeding enriched, ranked, and explained candidate data back into the hiring workflow.

docs.title: 5.1 GDPR Implications for Cloud-Based CV Processing
docs.text:
CVs are among the most data-dense documents an organization routinely processes. A single CV typically contains extensive personal data under GDPR Art. 4(1): full name, home address, phone number, email address, date of birth, employment history, educational background, professional certifications, and often photographs. Beyond these identifiers, CVs may inadvertently reveal Art. 9 special category data — racial or ethnic origin (from name, nationality, or language proficiency), political opinions (from organizational affiliations), religious beliefs (from education or volunteer work), health status (from employment gaps or disability accommodations), and trade union membership.

When an organization uses a cloud-based recruitment platform to process CVs, it creates a controller-processor relationship under GDPR Art. 28, requiring a formal Data Processing Agreement (DPA) with the vendor. This is the minimum — the practical reality is far more complex. A typical cloud-based ATS relies on 15 to 30 sub-processors: cloud infrastructure providers (AWS, Azure, GCP), analytics services, AI model providers, email delivery platforms, search indexing services, CDN providers, and monitoring tools. Each sub-processor in this chain is a GDPR risk vector — any one of them can be the source of a data breach, an unauthorized transfer, or a compliance failure.

A particularly insidious risk involves model training on customer data. Some AI vendors use candidate data submitted by their customers to improve their models. This means candidate data from Company A could influence the scoring algorithms applied to Company B's candidates — a practice that raises serious questions under the purpose limitation principle (Art. 5(1)(b)) and potentially constitutes unauthorized further processing.

GDPR Art. 22 imposes restrictions on automated decision-making that produces legal or similarly significant effects. If an AI system automatically rejects candidates without meaningful human review, candidates have the right to obtain human intervention, express their point of view, and contest the decision. Many cloud platforms offer automated rejection workflows that may not satisfy this requirement.

Art. 35 requires a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) for processing that involves systematic and extensive profiling of natural persons with decisions that produce legal effects — a description that precisely matches AI-powered candidate screening.

Data breach notification obligations under Art. 33–34 impose a 72-hour notification window that applies to every processor and sub-processor in the chain, creating coordination complexity that scales with the number of third parties involved.

VICA's position on GDPR is structural, not contractual. Candidate data never leaves the organization's perimeter. There is no Art. 28 processor agreement needed because there is no processor. There is no sub-processor chain because there are no sub-processors. There are no cross-border transfer mechanisms required because data does not cross borders. Agenderization (gender-neutral language replacement) occurs before any AI processing, reducing gender bias risk. The organization retains complete control as the sole data controller operating on its own infrastructure.

docs.title: 5.2 EU AI Act — Employment AI as "High-Risk"
docs.text:
The EU AI Act (Regulation 2024/1689) entered into force in August 2024, establishing the world's first comprehensive legal framework for artificial intelligence. Employment AI is explicitly listed as high-risk under Annex III, point 4(a):

"AI systems intended to be used for recruitment or selection of natural persons, in particular to place targeted job advertisements, to analyse and filter job applications, and to evaluate candidates in the course of interviews or tests."

This classification subjects all AI-powered recruitment screening tools — including VICA and every competitor in this analysis — to mandatory requirements:

  • Risk management system (Art. 9): Providers must establish a continuous process for identifying, analyzing, evaluating, and mitigating risks throughout the AI system's lifecycle. This includes testing to ensure the system performs consistently for its intended purpose.

  • Data governance (Art. 10): Training, validation, and testing datasets must meet quality criteria including relevance, representativeness, and freedom from errors. For high-risk systems, data governance must address potential biases that could lead to discriminatory outcomes.

  • Technical documentation (Art. 11): Detailed documentation of the system's design, development, capabilities, and limitations must be maintained. This documentation must be sufficient for authorities to assess compliance.

  • Transparency (Art. 13): High-risk AI systems must be designed to ensure that their operation is sufficiently transparent to enable deployers to interpret the system's output and use it appropriately. VICA's citation-based evidence extraction, 4-level proficiency assessments, justification scoring, and per-skill match analysis directly satisfy this requirement. A recruiter using VICA can trace every recommendation back to specific CV passages — a level of transparency no cloud competitor currently provides.

  • Human oversight (Art. 14): The system must allow effective human oversight, including the ability to correctly interpret output, decide not to use or override the system, and intervene or halt operation. VICA's recruiter validation loop — where recruiters can reorder skills by priority, toggle required/preferred status, and review citation-level evidence before any ranking — is architecturally built-in human oversight, not a compliance checkbox.

  • Accuracy, robustness, cybersecurity (Art. 15): Systems must achieve appropriate levels of accuracy and be resilient to errors and inconsistencies. VICA's built-in validation framework (precision, recall, F1, Accuracy@K) provides documented accuracy metrics that can be presented to regulators.

The compliance timeline requires new AI systems to comply by August 2025 and existing systems by August 2027. Penalties for non-compliance reach up to EUR 35 million or 7% of global annual turnover, whichever is higher.

VICA's advantage under the EU AI Act is threefold: open-source code is auditable by definition, satisfying transparency and documentation requirements; the built-in validation framework provides accuracy documentation; and on-premise deployment simplifies the cybersecurity scope by eliminating internet-facing attack surfaces and third-party infrastructure dependencies.

docs.title: 5.3 Data Residency and Cross-Border Transfer
docs.text:
The legal landscape for cross-border data transfers remains unstable and increasingly restrictive:

Schrems II (CJEU, July 2020) invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield and imposed a case-by-case assessment obligation on Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs), requiring organizations to evaluate whether the legal framework of the destination country provides adequate protection. For transfers to the United States, the CJEU found that US surveillance laws (FISA Section 702, Executive Order 12333) undermined the essentially equivalent protection standard.

The EU-US Data Privacy Framework (adopted July 2023) provides a new adequacy basis for EU-to-US transfers, based on Executive Order 14086 and a new Data Protection Review Court. However, this framework remains legally contested — privacy advocates have signaled challenges, and the European Parliament has expressed concerns about its durability. A "Schrems III" challenge could invalidate this framework, just as Schrems I and II invalidated its predecessors.

The US CLOUD Act (2018) grants US law enforcement the ability to compel US-headquartered companies to produce data stored anywhere globally, regardless of where the data center is physically located. This means that using AWS, Azure, or GCP — even with EU-located data centers — does not eliminate the risk of US government access to candidate data. This is a structural issue that data center location alone cannot resolve.

Country-specific data residency requirements further complicate the landscape: Germany requires Works Council co-determination for AI systems affecting employees; France maintains active CNIL enforcement with substantial fines; China's PIPL mandates data localization for certain categories of personal information; Russia's Federal Law 242-FZ requires Russian citizens' personal data to be stored on servers physically located in Russia.

VICA's position eliminates all cross-border transfer questions. Data physically resides on company-owned hardware within the organization's jurisdiction. There is no US-headquartered processor in the data processing chain. No adequacy decision, no SCCs, no transfer impact assessment — the entire compliance category is removed by architecture.

docs.title: 5.4 Notable Data Breaches and Privacy Incidents in HR Tech
docs.text:
Year Incident Impact 2017 Indeed — resumes publicly accessible via URL manipulation Unknown number of resumes exposed 2019 Ladders — unsecured database 13.7M user records exposed 2020 Monster.com — resumes on unsecured server Resumes from 2014–2017 exposed 2021 LinkedIn — mass scraping 700M user profiles scraped and sold 2021 HireVue — EPIC FTC complaint AI facial analysis concerns; feature discontinued 2022 Kronos/UKG — ransomware Payroll disruption for thousands of employers 2023 MOVEit breach — affected Zellis (HR/payroll) BBC, British Airways, Aer Lingus employee data exposed 2024 France Travail (employment agency) 43M records exposed

Each incident involved cloud-hosted or internet-accessible systems. On-premise deployments like VICA eliminate the attack surface of internet-facing candidate databases. While no system is immune to all threats, removing internet accessibility for candidate data eliminates the most common and highest-impact attack vectors — unauthorized access via URL manipulation, unsecured databases, mass scraping, and supply chain attacks through third-party file transfer services.

docs.title: 5.5 US Regulatory Landscape
docs.text:
While the EU leads in comprehensive AI regulation, US jurisdictions are enacting targeted legislation that increasingly constrains automated employment decisions:

  • NYC Local Law 144 (effective January 2023): Requires mandatory bias audits by independent auditors for any automated employment decision tool (AEDT) used in hiring or promotion within New York City. Results must be publicly posted. Non-compliance carries fines of \(500–\)1,500 per violation.

  • Illinois BIPA (Biometric Information Privacy Act): Requires informed consent before collecting biometric data, with a private right of action. Directly relevant for video interview AI platforms like HireVue. Settlements have exceeded $200M (Facebook, 2021).

  • Colorado AI Act (signed 2024): Requires deployers of high-risk AI systems in employment to conduct impact assessments, provide notice to individuals, and allow appeals. Closely mirrors EU AI Act concepts.

  • EEOC guidance (2023): The Equal Employment Opportunity Commission issued guidance clarifying that AI and algorithmic tools used in employment decisions are subject to Title VII anti-discrimination requirements, regardless of whether the employer or a vendor designed the tool.

VICA's advantage in the US regulatory context: VICA performs no biometric processing (eliminating BIPA exposure), its open-source algorithm is directly auditable for bias audit requirements under Local Law 144 and the Colorado AI Act, and its built-in validation metrics (precision, recall, F1 by candidate demographics) provide the data foundation for EEOC-compliant disparate impact analysis.

docs.title: 6.2 Why Explainability Matters for Consulting Firms
docs.text:
Partner/MD review. In consulting firms, senior leadership — partners, managing directors, practice leads — make final staffing decisions. They expect justification, not scores. A partner reviewing candidates for a critical client engagement will not accept "85% match" as sufficient reasoning. VICA's reports show exactly which CV passages support each skill assessment, at what proficiency level, and with what justification strength. This maps directly to how senior professionals already evaluate candidates: by reviewing evidence.

Legal defensibility. Employment discrimination claims require documented, auditable reasoning for hiring decisions. When a rejected candidate challenges a decision, the organization must demonstrate that the selection process was based on legitimate, non-discriminatory criteria applied consistently. VICA's open-source algorithm combined with citation-level evidence creates a defensible audit trail — every skill assessment can be traced to specific CV text, every ranking can be reproduced, and the algorithm itself can be independently reviewed.

Client staffing. Consulting firms do not hire for internal roles alone — they staff client projects, often with the client having visibility into or approval over proposed team members. This means the consulting firm must explain candidate selection to their clients with evidence: "This candidate has 5 years of SAP implementation experience — here are the specific projects and roles from their CV." VICA's citation-level output directly supports this client-facing justification.

EU AI Act Art. 13 compliance. The regulation states: "High-risk AI systems shall be designed and developed in such a way as to ensure that their operation is sufficiently transparent to enable deployers to interpret the system's output and use it appropriately." VICA satisfies this requirement by design — every output is interpretable because every output is grounded in specific, cited evidence from the candidate's own documents.

Recruiter trust. Recruiters are more likely to adopt AI tools they can verify and override. Systems that produce unexplained scores create a trust deficit: recruiters either blindly follow the score (abdicating professional judgment) or ignore it entirely (negating the investment). VICA's recruiter validation loop — where recruiters order skills by priority, toggle required/preferred status, and review citation evidence — keeps humans in control. The AI augments the recruiter's judgment with evidence; it does not replace it.

docs.title: Strengths
docs.text:
• Complete data sovereignty — candidate data never leaves organizational infrastructure; no third-party processors, no sub-processor chains, no cross-border transfers
  • Citation-level explainability — unmatched by any competitor; 4-level proficiency assessment, verbatim CV evidence, 4-level justification scoring, and targeted interview question generation per candidate-skill match
  • SPEA2 multi-objective ranking — mathematically principled Pareto-optimal candidate selection, avoiding the information loss of collapsing multiple criteria into a single score
  • Open-source — fully auditable by regulators, customers, and the community; zero vendor lock-in; customizable to organizational needs
  • Built-in validation framework — precision, recall, F1, and Accuracy@K metrics computed natively; no competitor offers this as a standard feature
  • ESCO-compatible taxonomy with hierarchical skill matching (parent/child relationships) and fuzzy matching (RapidFuzz) for synonym and abbreviation handling
  • Agenderization — systematic gender-neutral language replacement before any AI processing, reducing gender bias in skill extraction and matching
  • Cost — zero recurring license fees; one-time hardware investment with sub-14-month payback versus cloud alternatives

docs.title: Weaknesses
docs.text:
• Requires internal DevOps capability for deployment, configuration, and ongoing maintenance — not a turnkey SaaS product
  • JSON file storage — current persistence layer is not enterprise-grade; lacks the transactional guarantees, concurrent access management, and scalability of PostgreSQL or similar RDBMS
  • No native ATS workflow — no interview scheduling, offer management, onboarding, or career site functionality
  • Limited integrations — no connectors for job boards, LinkedIn Recruiter, email platforms, or HRIS systems
  • Single-GPU constraint limits processing throughput for very high-volume hiring scenarios (>20K CVs/year without hardware scaling)
  • No commercial support — community-driven project without enterprise SLAs, guaranteed response times, or dedicated account management

docs.title: Opportunities
docs.text:
• EU AI Act enforcement (2025–2027) will mandate transparency and human oversight for all employment AI — VICA is compliant today while competitors face costly retrofitting
  • GDPR enforcement intensifying — cumulative fines exceeded EUR 4.5B by 2024, with increasing regulatory focus on automated profiling and cross-border data transfers in employment contexts
  • On-premise AI demand growing in regulated industries — financial services, government, healthcare, and defense organizations increasingly require data sovereignty guarantees that cloud vendors cannot provide
  • Consulting firms as beachhead market — a high-value, skill-intensive, privacy-conscious segment where explainability directly maps to business operations (client staffing justification)
  • Complement existing ATS platforms — integration partnerships with iCIMS, Greenhouse, Bullhorn, and others position VICA as an AI screening module rather than a competitive replacement
  • Model upgrades — larger open-source models (Llama 3.1 70B, Qwen2.5-VL-7B) can improve extraction quality without architecture changes; the system is model-agnostic by design
  • Multi-GPU scaling for enterprise throughput — adding a second GPU eliminates the single-GPU switching constraint and enables concurrent OCR and LLM processing

docs.title: Threats
docs.text:
• Cloud vendors adding on-premise options — SAP BTP, Azure Arc, and AWS Outposts enable hybrid deployment models that could partially address data sovereignty concerns while maintaining vendor ecosystems
  • Competitors adding explainability — Eightfold.ai, HireVue, and others are responding to regulatory pressure by adding explainability features, potentially narrowing VICA's differentiation
  • Rapid AI model evolution — the competitive advantage of specific models degrades quickly; maintaining state-of-the-art extraction quality requires continuous model evaluation and updates
  • "Good enough" cloud solutions for organizations not under direct regulatory pressure — many firms will accept lower explainability and cloud residency if the workflow is frictionless
  • Internal resistance to self-hosted solutions — IT teams may perceive on-premise AI as operationally complex compared to managed SaaS, even when TCO favors self-hosting
  • Market consolidation — large vendors acquiring AI-native players (Ceridian acquired Ideal, Employ Inc. consolidated Lever/Jobvite/JazzHR) reduces the addressable market and increases competitive resources

docs.title: 9.1 Recommended Market Position
docs.text:
"The only on-premise AI recruitment screener with citation-level explainability — built for EU AI Act compliance."

This positioning occupies an uncontested intersection: on-premise deployment, AI-powered screening, and deep explainability. No current competitor claims all three. The EU AI Act reference anchors the positioning to a concrete, time-bound regulatory driver rather than abstract privacy concerns.

docs.title: 9.2 Target Segment
docs.text:
• Primary: EU-based medium-to-large consulting firms (500–5,000 employees) with 50–200 recruiters. These organizations combine high-volume hiring with skill-intensive staffing requirements, client-facing justification needs, and direct EU AI Act exposure. The consulting sector's emphasis on evidence-based decisions maps naturally to VICA's citation-level output.

  • Secondary: Regulated industries (financial services, government agencies, healthcare organizations, defense contractors) with strict data sovereignty requirements. These organizations face sector-specific regulations layered on top of GDPR and the EU AI Act, making on-premise processing a compliance necessity rather than a preference.

  • Tertiary: Privacy-conscious mid-market companies in any sector that are proactively preparing for regulatory compliance or that have experienced data sovereignty concerns with cloud vendors.

docs.title: 9.3 Go-to-Market Strategy
docs.text:
1. Open-source adoption: DevOps and engineering teams discover VICA through GitHub, Docker Hub, or AI/HR tech communities. They deploy, evaluate, and validate the system internally — building organizational confidence before any commercial commitment. The open-source model eliminates procurement friction and allows technical teams to champion the tool based on demonstrated value.

  2. ATS partnerships: Integrate with existing ATS platforms (iCIMS, Greenhouse, Bullhorn, SmartRecruiters) as an AI screening module. Rather than competing with established hiring workflows, VICA enhances them — receiving candidate applications from the ATS, performing AI-powered screening and ranking, and returning enriched candidate profiles with citations and recommendations. This partnership model expands distribution while reinforcing VICA's positioning as a specialized intelligence layer.

  3. Managed service: Offer professional deployment, configuration, and maintenance services for organizations that lack internal DevOps capacity or prefer a supported deployment. This addresses the primary adoption barrier (operational complexity) without sacrificing the on-premise architecture. Pricing can follow a fixed-fee or retainer model, maintaining cost predictability.

  4. Consulting firm network effects: The consulting industry operates on peer awareness and competitive benchmarking. Once one major consulting firm adopts VICA for compliant AI screening, peer firms face competitive pressure to match that capability — particularly as EU AI Act deadlines approach. Early adopters become reference cases that accelerate subsequent adoption within the sector.

docs.title: VICA Production Deployment — Cost Estimation and ROI Analysis
docs.text: Investment analysis for deploying VICA in a medium-big consulting company. Target environment: 500-5,000 employees, 50-200 recruiters, 5,000-20,000 CVs processed per year. All figures in USD. Estimates as of Q1 2026.

docs.title: 1. Executive Summary
docs.text:
Investment Overview - Year 1 total investment: $115,000 - $265,000 (hardware + development + deployment) - Annual recurring cost (Years 2-5): $33,000 - $75,000 - 5-Year TCO: $247,000 - $565,000 - Comparable cloud solution 5-Year TCO: $750,000 - \(2,000,000 - **ROI**: 150-400% over 5 years - **Break-even**: 8-14 months from go-live - **Recruiter hours saved**: 12,500 - 75,000 over 5 years - **Cost per CV screen**: <\)8 (vs \(25-\)40 manual)

Consulting firms screen thousands of CVs per year for both project staffing and external recruitment. Whether matching internal consultants to client engagements or evaluating external candidates for open positions, the screening process is labor-intensive and time-sensitive. Manual screening costs 24-45 minutes per CV when done thoroughly — reading the document, identifying relevant skills, comparing against job requirements, documenting the assessment, and preparing interview questions. At scale, this represents a significant portion of recruiter capacity that could be directed toward higher-value activities such as candidate engagement, client relationship management, and strategic workforce planning.

VICA reduces the active recruiter time per CV from 24-45 minutes to 1-3 minutes by automating the read-extract-match-document pipeline. The system processes CVs through local OCR, extracts skills with proficiency levels using a local LLM, matches candidates to job requirements using a multi-objective evolutionary algorithm (SPEA2), and generates citation-backed evidence reports with interview questions. Crucially, every skill assessment is linked to verbatim quotes from the original CV — a level of traceability that manual screening cannot match consistently, especially under time pressure.

The open-source stack eliminates recurring software license fees entirely. VICA runs on-premise using free and open-source components (FastAPI, React, Ollama, vLLM, Docker), meaning the primary investment is human work for integration, customization, and ongoing maintenance. This front-loaded cost structure contrasts sharply with cloud AI screening platforms that charge \(150,000-\)400,000 per year in recurring license fees. Over a 5-year horizon, VICA delivers 47-83% lower total cost of ownership while keeping all candidate data on-premise — a material advantage for GDPR compliance and EU AI Act readiness.

docs.title: Hardware Justification
docs.text:
VICA's GPU orchestrator automatically switches between the OCR service (Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct on vLLM, ~6GB VRAM) and the LLM service (llama3.1:8b on Ollama, ~8GB VRAM) via Docker API calls. Both models fit comfortably within 24GB VRAM, making the RTX 4090 a cost-effective entry point.

Tier 1 (RTX 4090) handles up to approximately 25 CVs/hour with sequential GPU switching. Processing is serialized: OCR runs first (~30s/page), then the GPU switches to the LLM for skill extraction (~30-60s per candidate). This is sufficient for 5,000 CVs/year with batch processing during business hours.

Tier 2 (A100 40GB) opens the door to significantly larger models. The llama3.1:70b model (~40GB VRAM) delivers an estimated 30-40% improvement in extraction accuracy over the 8b variant — particularly for nuanced skill identification, ambiguous CV formatting, and multilingual documents. Alternatively, the A100 can run both OCR and LLM models simultaneously (eliminating the GPU switching overhead entirely) if using the smaller models.

Tier 3 (dual A100 80GB or H100 80GB) enables running OCR and LLM on separate GPUs simultaneously, effectively doubling throughput. This tier also supports frontier-class models such as Qwen2.5-VL-72B for OCR or DeepSeek-V3 for extraction, pushing quality toward cloud API parity while maintaining full on-premise control.

Memory: VICA's in-memory store with JSON file persistence is lightweight. 64GB RAM handles approximately 50,000 candidate records comfortably. 128GB+ allows headroom for growth, OS caching, and concurrent processing buffers.

Storage: The average CV PDF is approximately 200KB. Processing 20,000 CVs/year generates roughly 4GB/year of document storage. AI models are cached in Docker volumes — approximately 15GB total for the current model stack (Qwen2.5-VL-3B + llama3.1:8b), scaling to approximately 80GB for 70B-class models. NVMe storage ensures fast model loading on GPU switch events.

Rank analysis from validation stats Skill coverage > 85% % of job skills where at least one candidate matches Skill coverage analysis per job posting

docs.title: Success Criteria for Go-Live
docs.text:
• System processes 95%+ of uploaded CVs without errors
  • F1 score exceeds 0.65 in validation against historical hires
  • System handles peak load (50+ concurrent CVs) without degradation
  • Recruiter satisfaction survey averages above 4.0/5.0 in pilot
  • All critical integrations (ATS, SSO) operational
  • Monitoring and alerting in place (GPU utilization, system health, error rates)
  • Backup and disaster recovery tested
  • Security audit passed with no critical findings

This analysis is prepared for executive review. Cost estimates are based on Western European labor rates (EUR 700-900/day), Q1 2026 hardware pricing, and industry benchmarks for recruitment process efficiency. Actual figures may vary based on organizational specifics, vendor negotiations, and scope changes. A 15% contingency is included in Year 1 estimates.

docs.title: Hardware Note
docs.text:
This setup assumes a single GPU (e.g. RTX 5070). The OCR model (Qwen2.5-VL via vLLM) and LLM (Ollama) cannot run simultaneously. The backend auto-orchestrates GPU switching:

  1. OCR phase — Qwen2.5-VL on the GPU (parse CVs and JD documents)
  2. LLM phase — Ollama on the GPU (skill extraction, JD skill extraction, match analysis)

docs.title: Software Setup
docs.text: Service Default URL Purpose Backend http://localhost:8000 FastAPI server Frontend http://localhost:5173 Vite React dev server OCR (Qwen2.5-VL) http://localhost:8001 Vision OCR for PDF/image parsing (vLLM) Ollama http://localhost:11434 LLM for skill extraction/analysis

docs.title: Start Order
docs.text:
# Terminal 1: Backend (always running)
cd vica
python run_backend.py

# Terminal 2: Frontend (always running)
cd vica/frontend
npm run dev

docs.title: Prepare Test Files
docs.text:
• 2-3 CV PDFs (real or sample)
  • 1 job description (can paste text directly)

docs.title: Phase A: Upload CVs (no GPU needed)
docs.text:
1. Open http://localhost:5173
  2. Verify the stepper shows 4 steps: Upload CVs > Job Description > Validate Skills > Match & Reports
  3. Step 1 should be active (blue circle)
  4. Click "Upload CVs" — select 2-3 PDF files (multi-select supported)
  5. Verify all files appear in the table with gray "Uploaded" badges
  6. Progress bar shows "0/N ready"
  7. The "Next" button should be disabled (no candidates are ready yet)

docs.title: Phase B: Parse CVs (GPU: OCR auto-switch)
docs.text:
1. The backend will automatically stop Ollama and start the OCR server (Qwen2.5-VL) when needed
  2. In the UI, check the GPU status badge — click "Refresh" if needed, should show "GPU: OCR active"
  3. Click "Parse All (OCR)"
  4. Wait for completion — status message shows "N CVs parsed"
  5. All candidates should now show yellow "Parsed" badges
  6. Progress bar updates

docs.title: Phase C: Extract Skills (GPU: LLM auto-switch)
docs.text:
1. The backend will automatically stop the OCR server and load the Ollama model when needed
  2. Click the GPU "Refresh" button — should show "GPU: LLM active"
  3. Click "Extract Skills (LLM)"
  4. Wait for completion (this takes longer — LLM processes each CV)
  5. Status message shows "N skills extracted"
  6. All candidates show green "Ready" badges
  7. Progress bar shows "N/N ready"
  8. The "Next" button is now enabled
  9. Click "Next: Job Description"

docs.title: Phase D: Create Job Description (GPU: Ollama stays on)
docs.text:
Keep Ollama running — the next steps need it for JD skill extraction.

  1. Stepper shows step 2 active
  2. Option A: Click "Upload JD (PDF/image)" if you have a JD file
  3. Note: this needs the OCR server — the backend will auto-switch GPU if needed. Or just paste text instead.
  4. Option B (recommended for testing): Paste job description text into the textarea
  5. Click "Create Job"
  6. Verify green badge shows "Job #1 created"
  7. Click "Next: Validate Skills"

docs.title: Phase E: Validate & Order Skills (GPU: Ollama)
docs.text:
1. Stepper shows step 3 active
  2. Skills are auto-extracted from the JD by the LLM — wait for the list to appear
  3. Verify the skill list shows with:
  4. Drag handles on the left
  5. Checkboxes (checked = required)
  6. Green left border for required skills

  7. Delete (X) button on the right

  8. Test drag-and-drop: Drag a skill to a new position

  9. Test toggle: Uncheck a skill's "Required" checkbox
  10. Badge should change from "Required" (green) to "Preferred" (gray)
  11. Left border changes from green to gray
  12. Test delete: Click the X button on a skill — it should disappear
  13. Verify the header counts update (e.g., "5 required, 2 preferred")
  14. Click "Save Skills" — verify "Saved" badge appears
  15. Click "Next: Match & Reports"

docs.title: Phase F: Match & Reports (GPU: Ollama)
docs.text:
1. Stepper shows step 4 active
  2. Set "Top candidates" to 2 or 3
  3. Click "Run Match"
  4. Spinner shows "Ranking & Analyzing..." — this runs SPEA2 + LLM analysis
  5. Wait for results table to appear
  6. Verify the results show:
  7. Run number badge
  8. "N top" badge (number of analyzed candidates)
  9. "N total" badge (total candidates in the pool, if more than top)
  10. "N analyzed" badge

  11. Table with Rank, Candidate ID, R (SPEA2), Fitness score, Report buttons

  12. Test inline report: Click "Report" on a top candidate

  13. An iframe should expand below the row showing the full report

  14. Click "Hide" to collapse it

  15. Test new tab: Click the arrow button — report opens in a new browser tab

  16. Test remaining candidates list: Below the top results table, click "Show remaining N candidates"

  17. An expandable scrollable list appears with Rank, Candidate ID, R (SPEA2), Fitness, and CV button
  18. These candidates have no report (only top-K are analyzed)
  19. Click "Hide remaining N candidates" to collapse the list
  20. The list has a max height of 320px and scrolls vertically

docs.title: Phase G: Verify Report Content
docs.text:
In the report (inline or new tab), check:

Check Expected Fit score NOT present — no "X/10" numerical score anywhere Recommendation Badge visible: Strong Yes / Yes / Maybe / No Overall fit 1-2 sentence assessment text Strengths Bulleted list (green) Risks / Gaps Bulleted list (red) Required Skills table Each skill shows Status (check/cross), Match level badge, Evidence CV Citations label Each skill with evidence shows "CV Citations:" header Full citations ALL literal quotes from the CV shown (not truncated to 2-3) Preferred Skills table Same structure as required Transferable Skills Card layout with skill name, relation, reasoning Interview Questions Each question has a category badge: blue "Technical" or orange "Verification" Question types Mix of verification (probing weak claims) and technical depth (architecture, trade-offs) Priority badges Each question shows priority: high/medium/low Additional Skills Skills not in JD requirements listed with proficiency Candidate Profile Years of experience, education level, field of study (when extracted)

docs.title: Phase H: Navigation Tests
docs.text:
1. Click "Back" from step 4 to step 3 — state should be preserved
  2. Click "Back" from step 3 to step 2 — job description text still there
  3. Click "Back" from step 2 to step 1 — candidates still listed and ready
  4. Click a completed step circle in the stepper — should navigate there directly
  5. Try clicking a future step — should not navigate unless current step is complete

docs.title: Phase I: Sidebar & Settings Tests
docs.text:
1. Sidebar shows Jobs, Candidates, Validation Runs navigation links with counts
  2. Sidebar shows Quick Actions: New Job, New Validation Run
  3. Sidebar shows Overview stats: jobs ready, total candidates, validations done
  4. Click Settings in sidebar — navigates to Settings page
  5. Settings page shows GPU Management with activate/release controls
  6. Settings page shows LLM Configuration (model, URL, connectivity status)
  7. Settings page shows OCR Configuration (URL, health)
  8. Settings page shows About section with version info

docs.title: Phase J: Search & Filter Tests
docs.text:
1. On Jobs page, type in search bar — filters jobs by title in real-time
  2. Use status dropdown to filter "Skills ready" vs "Needs setup"
  3. Click sort buttons (Date, Title, CVs) — list reorders; click again to toggle asc/desc
  4. "Showing X of Y jobs" counter appears when filters are active
  5. On Validation Runs page, search by title — filters runs in real-time
  6. Use type filter (Historical / Supervised) and status filter (Setup, Completed, etc.)
  7. Sort buttons (Date, Title, Status) work correctly
  8. Clear search and reset filters — full list reappears

docs.title: No GPU services running
docs.text:
1. Stop both the OCR server and Ollama
  2. Upload a .txt file as CV — should parse without OCR (text files don't need it)
  3. GPU badge shows "GPU: no service detected"
  4. Helpful hints appear explaining which service to start

docs.title: LLM disabled
docs.text:
1. Set LLM_ENABLED=false in .env or environment
  2. Restart backend
  3. Skill extraction uses phrase matching fallback (no GPU needed)
  4. Match analysis uses fallback mode (no LLM analysis, basic binary matching)
  5. Report still renders but with limited data

docs.title: Empty states
docs.text:
1. No candidates uploaded — step 1 shows "No candidates yet" message
  2. Run match with 0 candidates — should show clear error message
  3. JD with no extractable skills — skill editor shows "No skills extracted" message

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Problem Solution "Parse All" fails Check OCR server is running: curl http://localhost:8001/health (backend auto-starts it, check logs) "Extract Skills" fails Check Ollama is running: curl http://localhost:11434/v1/models Both services needed at once Not possible with single GPU. The backend auto-switches between OCR and LLM as needed Skill extraction returns 0 skills Check Ollama has the model loaded: ollama list should show llama3.2 Report shows "No evidence found" LLM may not have run analysis — check that "Run Match" completed with "N analyzed" JD skill extraction empty Ensure the job description text is substantial enough for the LLM to extract skills Frontend not connecting Check Vite proxy config or CORS — backend should be on port 8000

docs.title: AI & ML Engineering
docs.text: Technical documentation for VICA's AI pipeline — from skill extraction through multi-objective ranking to semantic analysis.

docs.title: Pipeline Overview
docs.text:
graph TB
    subgraph Ingestion["1. Document Ingestion"]
        OCR["OCR (Qwen2.5-VL-3B)<br/>PDF/Image → Text"]
        AGD["Agenderization<br/>Regex-based"]
        OCR --> AGD
    end

    subgraph Extraction["2. Skill Extraction"]
        DEEP["Deep LLM Extraction<br/>llama3.1:8b"]
        NORM["Taxonomy Normalization<br/>ESCO + RapidFuzz"]
        VEC["Binary Skill Vector<br/>has/doesn't have"]
        DEEP --> NORM --> VEC
    end

    subgraph Ranking["3. Multi-Objective Ranking"]
        MATRIX["Candidate × Skill Matrix<br/>Polars/Pandas"]
        SPEA["SPEA2<br/>Pareto Dominance + Strength"]
        MATRIX --> SPEA
    end

    subgraph Analysis["4. Semantic Analysis"]
        MATCH["Per-Candidate LLM Analysis<br/>Match levels, evidence, questions"]
        REPORT["Report Generation<br/>Jinja2 templates"]
        MATCH --> REPORT
    end

    Ingestion --> Extraction --> Ranking --> Analysis

docs.title: Scoring Flow
docs.text:
graph LR
    CV["CV Text"] --> DE["Deep Extraction"]
    DE --> PROF["Proficiency<br/>expert/proficient/<br/>familiar/mentioned"]
    DE --> JUST["Justification<br/>strong/moderate/<br/>weak/unsubstantiated"]
    DE --> EVID["Evidence<br/>Verbatim CV quotes"]
    DE --> BIN["Binary Vector<br/>1 or 0"]

    BIN --> SPEA2["SPEA2 Ranking<br/>(binary × weights)"]

    SPEA2 --> SEM["Semantic Analysis"]
    SEM --> ML["Match Level<br/>strong/partial/<br/>transferable/no_match"]
    SEM --> REC["Recommendation<br/>strong_yes/yes/<br/>maybe/no"]
    SEM --> IQ["Interview Questions<br/>verification + depth"]

SPEA2 uses binary skill presence only

Proficiency, justification, and evidence are not used in ranking — they exist for reports and post-match analysis.

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text: Document What it covers SPEA2 Algorithm Pareto dominance, strength, raw fitness, tie-breaking — theory and correctness analysis Prompt Templates Every LLM prompt, expected outputs, token limits, fallback behavior Scoring Rubrics All scoring scales, how they combine, SPEA2 weights Skill Normalization ESCO taxonomy, fuzzy matching, hierarchy, open vocabulary Validation Metrics Precision, Recall, F1, Accuracy@K, rank analysis, fitness distributions

docs.title: Prompt Templates Reference
docs.text: All LLM calls in VICA go through call_local_llm() in app/services/llm_client.py. Every call uses: - API: OpenAI-compatible (/v1/chat/completions) - Temperature: 0 (deterministic) - Provider: Local only (Ollama, vLLM, LM Studio)

docs.title: 1. CV Deep Skill Extraction
docs.text:
Source: app/services/skill_extraction.py:27 — DEEP_SKILL_EXTRACT_PROMPT

Purpose: Extract all professional/technical skills from a CV with structured evidence.

System message: The prompt itself (no separate system message).

User message: CV text (truncated to 32,000 chars).

Token limit: 16,384 output tokens. Timeout: 300s.

Prompt text:

You are an expert technical recruiter analyzing a CV. Extract ALL professional and technical skills you can identify.

For EACH skill, provide:
- "name": the canonical skill name (use standard industry names: "AWS" not "Amazon Web Services",
  "CI/CD" not "CI/CD pipelines", "JavaScript" not "Modern JavaScript").
  One skill per entry — split compound skills like "Python/Django" into separate entries
- "proficiency": one of "expert", "proficient", "familiar", or "mentioned"
  - expert: multiple years of hands-on use, led projects, deep knowledge demonstrated
  - proficient: clear practical experience, used in real projects
  - familiar: some exposure, coursework, or minor usage
  - mentioned: listed but no supporting evidence in the CV
- "evidence": array of ALL direct quotes from the CV that support this skill. CITATION RULES:
  - Quote FULL SENTENCES or full bullet points from the CV, not fragments
  - Include the surrounding context (company name, project, dates) when present in the same sentence or bullet
  - If a skill is mentioned across multiple sections (e.g. work experience AND projects),
    include quotes from EACH section
  - Copy the text EXACTLY as it appears in the CV — do not paraphrase or summarize
  - Each quote should be long enough to stand alone as evidence (typically 1-3 complete sentences)
- "context": brief description of WHERE/HOW the skill was used
  (e.g. "used in backend development at CompanyX for 2 years")
- "justification": one of "strong", "moderate", "weak", "unsubstantiated"
  - strong: multiple concrete examples, projects, or years of experience
  - moderate: at least one concrete example or project
  - weak: mentioned in skills list or implied but limited evidence
  - unsubstantiated: claimed but contradicted or no evidence at all

Return ONLY a JSON object:
{"skills": [{"name": "...", "proficiency": "...", "evidence": ["..."],
  "context": "...", "justification": "..."}, ...]}

Be thorough — extract every skill you can find, including soft skills if clearly evidenced.
Do NOT invent skills that are not in the CV.

Expected output:

{
  "skills": [
    {
      "name": "Python",
      "proficiency": "expert",
      "evidence": ["Led migration of legacy Java services to Python 3.11 microservices at ACME Corp (2021-2023)"],
      "context": "Primary backend language at ACME Corp for 3+ years",
      "justification": "strong"
    }
  ]
}

Output field meanings:

Field Type Values Purpose name string Canonical skill name Used for taxonomy matching and binary vector proficiency string expert / proficient / familiar / mentioned Skill depth level (for reports only, not SPEA2) evidence string[] Exact CV quotes Shown in candidate reports as citations context string Where/how used Brief description for report justification string strong / moderate / weak / unsubstantiated Evidence quality (for reports only, not SPEA2)

Fallback: When LLM is unavailable, falls through to simple extraction (below), then phrase matching.

docs.title: 2. CV Simple Skill Extraction (Legacy)
docs.text:
Source: app/services/skill_extraction.py:18 — SKILL_EXTRACT_PROMPT

Purpose: Extract skill names only (no evidence/proficiency). Used as fallback when deep extraction fails.

Prompt text:

You extract a list of professional/technical skills from a CV text.
Return only a JSON object with a single key 'skills' and value being an array of skill names (strings).
No explanation, only valid JSON.

Expected output: {"skills": ["Python", "AWS", "Docker", ...]}

Token limit: Default. Timeout: Default.

docs.title: 3. JD Skill Extraction
docs.text:
Source: app/services/jd_extraction.py:26 — JD_SKILLS_PROMPT

Purpose: Extract ordered skills from a job description, classifying each as required or preferred.

User message: JD text (truncated to 16,000 chars).

Prompt text:

You extract professional/technical skills from a job description and classify each as
"required" or "preferred" based on the JD wording.

CLASSIFICATION RULES:
- "required": true — Skills from sections like 'Required qualifications', 'Core requirements',
  'Must have', 'Requirements', 'What you need', 'Minimum qualifications', or from the main
  responsibilities/duties section.
- "required": false — Skills from sections like 'Preferred', 'Strongly preferred',
  'Nice to have', 'Bonus', 'Plus', 'Desirable', 'Good to have', or 'Ideal candidate' sections.
- If a JD has NO clear section headers, classify skills that appear essential to the role as
  required and supplementary skills as preferred.

Order by importance (most important first).

CRITICAL RULES for skill names:
1. DECOMPOSE compound phrases into separate atomic skills.
   Example: "Modern JavaScript or TypeScript" → two entries: "JavaScript" and "TypeScript".
   Example: "Automated testing for backend and frontend code" → "Automated Testing".
   Example: "Asynchronous processing or task queues" → "Asynchronous Processing" and "Task Queues".
2. Use SHORT CANONICAL names — the standard industry name for the technology or skill.
   Do NOT include adjectives, qualifiers, or workflow descriptions.
   Example: "Cloud deployment workflows" → "Cloud".
   Example: "CI/CD pipelines" → "CI/CD".
   Example: "REST API design" → "REST APIs".
   Example: "Container orchestration" → "Kubernetes".
   Example: "NoSQL databases" → "NoSQL".
   Example: "Relational databases" → "SQL".
3. If a JD phrase maps to a well-known technology, use that name:
   "AWS services" → "AWS", "Google Cloud" → "GCP", "Microsoft Azure" → "Azure".
Return ONLY a JSON object: {"skills": [{"name": "...", "required": true/false}, ...]}.
No explanation, only valid JSON.

Expected output:

{"skills": [{"name": "Python", "required": true}, {"name": "Docker", "required": false}]}

Fallback: When LLM unavailable, uses _extract_jd_skills_regex() — phrase matching against the skill library.

Post-processing: After LLM extraction, normalize_jd_skills() applies: 1. Compound decomposition (_decompose_compound_skill): splits "X or Y" / "X and Y" into separate entries 2. Taxonomy resolution: maps to ESCO canonical names when possible 3. Qualifier stripping (_strip_qualifiers): removes noise suffixes/prefixes ("CI/CD pipelines" → "CI/CD") 4. Deduplication preserving order

docs.title: 3b. JD Requirements Extraction
docs.text:
Source: app/services/jd_extraction.py — JD_REQUIREMENTS_PROMPT

Purpose: Extract experience and education requirements from the JD (separate from skills).

Prompt text:

Extract the experience and education requirements from this job description.
Return ONLY a JSON object with these keys (use null if not found):
"min_years_experience" (float, minimum years of professional experience mentioned),
"education_level" (string, minimum required: one of "phd", "master", "bachelor", etc.),
"education_field" (string, required field of study if specified, or null).
No explanation, only valid JSON.

Expected output:

{"min_years_experience": 5.0, "education_level": "bachelor", "education_field": "Computer Science"}

Role in matching: These requirements are stored on the job and used as extra SPEA2 objectives during the match run. Candidates' temporal/education features (from prompt #6) are scored against these requirements.

Fallback: Regex-based extraction of "N+ years" patterns and degree keywords.

docs.title: 4. JD Generation (Testing Only)
docs.text:
Source: app/services/jd_extraction.py:14 — GENERATE_JD_SYSTEM + GENERATE_JD_USER

Purpose: Generate synthetic job descriptions for testing.

System message:

You are an expert HR writer. You produce complete, realistic tech job descriptions
suitable for real job postings. Output only the job description text, no preamble or meta-commentary.

User message:

Generate one complete tech job description (e.g. Senior Software Engineer, Data Engineer,
DevOps Engineer, or similar). Include: a clear job title at the top; a short 'About the role' paragraph;
'Responsibilities' (bullet list); 'Required qualifications' (bullet list with concrete technologies and years);
'Preferred qualifications' and 'Nice to have' (optional). Use realistic wording and common tech stack names
(e.g. Python, React, AWS, Kubernetes, PostgreSQL). Keep it professional and 300–600 words.

Token limit: 2,048. Timeout: 120s.

docs.title: 5. Candidate Analysis (Post-Match)
docs.text:
Source: app/services/skill_matching.py:22 — CANDIDATE_ANALYSIS_PROMPT

Purpose: Deep semantic analysis of a candidate against a job. Called once per candidate in the top-K after SPEA2 ranking.

System message: "You are an expert technical recruiter. Analyze candidates against job requirements. Return only valid JSON."

User message: The prompt below with variables filled in: - {required_skills}: comma-separated required skill names - {preferred_skills}: comma-separated preferred skill names - {candidate_skill_profile}: formatted deep skills with proficiency, evidence quotes, context - {cv_text}: agenderized CV text (truncated to 24,000 chars)

Token limit: 16,384. Timeout: 300s.

Prompt text:

You are an expert technical recruiter performing a deep analysis of a candidate
against a job description.

## Job Skills
Required skills (MUST have): {required_skills}
Preferred skills (NICE to have): {preferred_skills}

## Candidate's Skill Profile
{candidate_skill_profile}

## Candidate's CV Text
{cv_text}

Analyze this candidate thoroughly and return a JSON object with these sections:

### 1. skill_assessments
For EACH job skill (required AND preferred), assess the candidate:
- "skill": the job skill name
- "required": true/false
- "match_level": one of "strong_match", "partial_match", "transferable", "no_match"
- "evidence_summary": 1-2 sentence summary of relevant CV evidence
- "cv_quotes": array of ALL direct quotes from the CV supporting this skill (CITATION RULES apply)
- "related_candidate_skills": any candidate skills related to this job skill
- "confidence": "high", "medium", or "low"

### 2. transferable_skills
Skills the candidate has that are NOT in the job requirements but are relevant:
- "skill": candidate skill name
- "relevant_to": which job skill(s) it could complement
- "reasoning": why this skill is transferable

### 3. interview_questions
4-8 targeted questions in TWO categories:

A) VERIFICATION (category: "verification"):
   - Skills claimed but with little evidence
   - Proficiency possibly inflated
B) TECHNICAL DEPTH (category: "technical_depth"):
   - Design/architecture questions on their strongest skills
   - "How would you..." scenarios combining multiple skills
   - Edge cases, trade-offs, failure scenarios

Each: "skill", "question", "reason", "category", "priority" (high/medium/low)

### 4. summary
- "strengths": array of 2-4 key strengths
- "risks": array of 1-3 concerns
- "overall_fit": 1-2 sentence assessment
- "recommendation": one of "strong_yes", "yes", "maybe", "no"

Return ONLY valid JSON.

Expected output (4 sections):

Section Contents skill_assessments Per-skill: match_level, evidence_summary, cv_quotes, confidence transferable_skills Skills not in JD but relevant, with reasoning interview_questions 4-8 questions (verification + technical_depth), with priority summary Strengths, risks, overall_fit text, recommendation

Fallback: _fallback_analysis() builds assessments from deep_skills data using proficiency/justification mapping. No transferable skills. Generates verification questions for weak/unsubstantiated skills only. Returns recommendation: "maybe".

docs.title: 6. Temporal & Education Feature Extraction
docs.text:
Source: app/services/temporal_extraction.py:12 — PROMPT

Purpose: Extract years of experience and education level from CV text.

User message: CV text (truncated to 8,000 chars).

Token limit: 256. Timeout: Default.

Prompt text:

Extract temporal/work-experience and education features from the CV text.
Return ONLY a JSON object with these keys (use null if not found):
"years_of_experience" (float, total professional experience),
"years_worked_abroad" (float, work outside home country),
"years_in_current_role" (float, if inferrable),
"years_in_management" (float, if inferrable),
"education_level" (string, highest completed: one of "phd", "master", "bachelor",
  "associate", "diploma", "certificate", "high_school", or null),
"education_field" (string, field of study of highest degree, e.g. "Computer Science",
  "Mechanical Engineering", "Business Administration", or null).
No explanation, only valid JSON.

Expected output:

{"years_of_experience": 8.0, "years_worked_abroad": 2.0, "years_in_current_role": null, "years_in_management": 3.0, "education_level": "master", "education_field": "Computer Science"}

Fallback: Returns empty dict {} when LLM unavailable.

Education levels (ordered highest to lowest): phd, master, bachelor, associate, diploma, certificate, high_school.

Role in matching: When a JD specifies minimum years of experience or education level, these become extra SPEA2 objectives alongside skills — candidates who meet or exceed requirements score higher.

docs.title: 7. OCR Text Extraction
docs.text:
Source: app/services/ocr.py:21 — EXTRACT_TEXT_PROMPT

Purpose: Extract text from document images using vision model.

Model: Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct (via vLLM, separate from the main LLM).

Prompt text:

Extract all text from this document in reading order. Preserve structure (headings, lists) where visible.

Input: Base64-encoded image sent as image_url content type.

Token limit: 4,096 per page. Timeout: 60s per page.

Fallback: When OCR is unavailable, PDF parsing falls back to pdfplumber (text-based extraction only, no image support).
