docs.title: How to modify prompts
docs.text:
1. Edit the prompt variable in the source file
  2. No restart needed if using --reload (uvicorn watches for changes)
  3. Temperature is hardcoded at 0 — change in call_local_llm() if needed
  4. To add a new output field, update both the prompt text AND the corresponding _parse_* / _validate_* function

docs.title: Changing the model
docs.text: Set LLM_MODEL in .env. Any OpenAI-compatible model works. Larger models may improve extraction quality but increase latency. The prompts are designed for models in the 8B-70B range.

docs.title: 9. Fallback Behavior Summary
docs.text: Component LLM Available LLM Unavailable CV Skill Extraction Deep extraction (proficiency + evidence) Phrase matching against skill library JD Skill Extraction LLM with decomposition + normalization Regex phrase matching, all marked required Candidate Analysis Full semantic analysis (4 sections) Binary presence mapping, basic questions Temporal Features LLM extracts years Empty dict (no temporal data) OCR Qwen2.5-VL vision model pdfplumber (text PDFs only)

docs.title: Scoring System Reference
docs.text: This document defines every scoring scale in VICA, how they combine, and how they flow into SPEA2 ranking and candidate reports.

docs.title: 1. Proficiency Levels (CV Extraction)
docs.text:
Assigned by the LLM during deep skill extraction (app/services/skill_extraction.py:33).

Level Definition Typical CV evidence expert Multiple years of hands-on use, led projects, deep knowledge demonstrated "Led migration of...", "5+ years", "architected" proficient Clear practical experience, used in real projects "Developed features using...", "worked on X project" familiar Some exposure, coursework, or minor usage "Completed coursework in...", "assisted with..." mentioned Listed but no supporting evidence in the CV Appears in skills list only, no context

Used in: Reports (skill detail cards). NOT used in SPEA2 ranking — SPEA2 uses binary presence only.

docs.title: 2. Justification Levels (CV Extraction)
docs.text:
Assigned by the LLM alongside proficiency (app/services/skill_extraction.py:44). Measures evidence quality independently from proficiency depth.

Level Definition Example strong Multiple concrete examples, projects, or years of experience 3 projects mentioned, specific metrics cited moderate At least one concrete example or project One project or role described weak Mentioned in skills list or implied but limited evidence Listed in "Skills:" section only unsubstantiated Claimed but contradicted or no evidence at all Says "expert in X" with zero supporting detail

Used in: Reports, fallback analysis logic, interview question generation (weak/unsubstantiated skills trigger verification questions).

docs.title: 3. Match Levels (Candidate Analysis)
docs.text:
Assigned by the LLM during post-SPEA2 semantic analysis (app/services/skill_matching.py:41). Assessed per job skill per candidate.

Level Definition Report display strong_match Clear, direct evidence of this skill with real experience Green badge partial_match Some evidence but not deep enough, or related but not exact Yellow badge transferable Candidate has a closely related skill that could transfer Blue badge no_match No evidence of this or related skills Red badge

Normalization (_normalize_match_level at line 191): The LLM may return variations — the code normalizes by: - Replacing spaces/hyphens with underscores - Mapping keywords: "strong" → strong_match, "partial" → partial_match, "transfer" → transferable - Default: no_match

docs.title: 4. Recommendation Levels (Candidate Analysis)
docs.text:
Assigned in the summary section of candidate analysis (app/services/skill_matching.py:87).

Level Meaning strong_yes Excellent fit, proceed with interview — strong evidence across required skills yes Good fit, standard interview process maybe Mixed signals, needs clarification — some gaps or unsubstantiated claims no Not suitable — missing critical required skills

Normalization (_normalize_recommendation at line 270): Maps LLM variations using keyword matching. Default: maybe.

docs.title: 5. Confidence Levels
docs.text:
Assigned per skill assessment in candidate analysis (app/services/skill_matching.py:54).

Level Meaning high Clear, definitive assessment — strong evidence or clear absence medium Reasonably confident — some evidence but room for interpretation low Uncertain or ambiguous — limited CV data for this skill

docs.title: 6. Interview Question Categories & Priority
docs.text:
Generated in candidate analysis (app/services/skill_matching.py:63).

Categories:

Category Purpose When generated verification Probe skills claimed but with little evidence Weak/unsubstantiated justification, unclear proficiency technical_depth Test real depth on strongest skills Expert/proficient skills with strong evidence

Priority levels:

Priority Meaning high Critical to verify — unsubstantiated claims or core required skills medium Important to understand — moderate evidence gaps low Nice to explore — additional depth on strong skills

docs.title: 7. How Scores Feed Into SPEA2
docs.text:
Important: SPEA2 uses binary skill presence only. A candidate either has a skill (1) or doesn't (0). Proficiency, justification, match_level, and confidence are not used in ranking — they exist for the report only.

graph LR
    CV["CV Text"] --> DE["Deep Extraction"]
    DE --> SN["Skill Names"]
    SN --> TN["Taxonomy<br/>Normalization"]
    TN --> BV["Binary Vector<br/>has / doesn't have"]
    BV --> SP["SPEA2 Ranking<br/>(binary × weights)"]

    DE -.->|"for reports only"| PROF["Proficiency"]
    DE -.->|"for reports only"| JUST["Justification"]
    DE -.->|"for reports only"| EVID["Evidence"]

The richer data (proficiency, evidence, justification) flows into: - Candidate reports: detailed skill cards with evidence citations - Post-match analysis: semantic match_level, transferable skills, interview questions - Fallback analysis: maps proficiency + justification to match_level when LLM unavailable

docs.title: 8. SPEA2 Weights
docs.text:
Defined in app/services/spea2.py:12.

Base weights:

Skill type Base weight Required WEIGHT_REQUIRED = 2.0 Preferred WEIGHT_PREFERRED = 1.0

Position factor: Skills earlier in the recruiter's ordered list get higher weight.

position_factor = (n - position) / n    # position 0 = 1.0, last position ≈ 1/n
position_factor = max(position_factor, 0.1)   # minimum floor of 0.1

Combined weight per skill:

weight[j] = base_weight × position_factor

Example with 5 skills (3 required, 2 preferred):

Position Skill Required Base Pos factor Final weight 0 Python Yes 2.0 1.0 2.000 1 AWS Yes 2.0 0.8 1.600 2 Docker Yes 2.0 0.6 1.200 3 TypeScript No 1.0 0.4 0.400 4 Redis No 1.0 0.2 0.200

Objective per candidate:

objectives[i, j] = weight[j] × binary(candidate i has skill j)

SPEA2 then uses Pareto dominance over these weighted objectives to rank candidates. See SPEA2 Algorithm for the full theory.

docs.title: 9. Fallback Scoring
docs.text:
When LLM is unavailable for candidate analysis, _fallback_analysis() in app/services/skill_matching.py:298 maps existing deep_skills data to match levels:

Proficiency Justification → Match level expert or proficient strong or moderate strong_match any other strong or moderate partial_match any weak or unsubstantiated partial_match (skill not found) — no_match

Fallback also generates verification interview questions for every skill with weak or unsubstantiated justification (up to 6 questions). No transferable skills are detected. Summary recommendation defaults to "maybe" with overall_fit text: "Analysis performed without LLM — limited to binary skill matching."

docs.title: Skill Normalization Pipeline
docs.text: How raw skill mentions from CVs and job descriptions become canonical skills, how the ESCO taxonomy works, and how hierarchy enables partial matching.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
When VICA extracts a skill name (e.g., "Amazon Web Services" from a CV or "Cloud deployment workflows" from a JD), it needs to map it to a standard form so that CV skills and JD skills can be compared. This happens in three stages:

graph LR
    RAW["Raw Skill Mention"] --> EX{"1. Exact Match?"}
    EX -->|"Yes"| ESCO1["ESCO Canonical<br/>source: esco"]
    EX -->|"No"| FZ{"2. Fuzzy Match<br/>≥ 85%?"}
    FZ -->|"Yes"| ESCO2["ESCO Canonical<br/>source: esco"]
    FZ -->|"No"| OV["Keep as-is<br/>source: other"]

docs.title: ESCO Taxonomy
docs.text:
Source file: data/taxonomy/esco_skills.json

Structure: A JSON array of skill objects:

{
  "id": "skill_001",
  "preferred_label": "Python",
  "alt_labels": ["Python programming", "Python 3"],
  "parent": "skill_000"
}
Field Purpose id Unique identifier preferred_label The canonical name (used everywhere in VICA) alt_labels Alternative names that resolve to the same canonical parent ID of the parent skill (enables hierarchy)

docs.title: TaxonomyResolver
docs.text: Source: app/services/taxonomy.py:26

docs.title: Index Building
docs.text:
On initialization, builds three indexes:

  1. Label-to-preferred map: every preferred_label and alt_label (lowercased) → preferred_label
  2. Phrase list: all labels for fuzzy matching
  3. Hierarchy maps: _parent_of (child → parent name) and _children_of (parent → set of children)

docs.title: Resolution: resolve(raw_skill) → (canonical_name, source)
docs.text:
1. Exact match:  raw_skill.lower() in label_to_preferred?
                 → Yes: return (preferred_label, "esco")

2. Fuzzy match:  rapidfuzz.process.extractOne(raw_skill, phrases, scorer=ratio)
                 → Score ≥ 85: return (preferred_label, "esco")

3. Open vocab:   → return (raw_skill, "other")

Fuzzy threshold: 85 (configurable via fuzz_threshold parameter). Uses rapidfuzz.fuzz.ratio scorer.

Example resolutions:

Raw input Resolution Source "Python" "Python" esco (exact) "python programming" "Python" esco (exact via alt_label) "Pythoon" "Python" esco (fuzzy, score ~91) "FastAPI" "FastAPI" other (not in taxonomy)

docs.title: Hierarchical Matching
docs.text:
Source: app/services/taxonomy.py:93 — get_related_skills(canonical_name)

Returns the skill itself + its parent + its children. This enables partial matching in SPEA2:

graph TD
    CLOUD["Cloud (parent)"] --> AWS["AWS (child)"]
    CLOUD --> AZURE["Azure (child)"]
    CLOUD --> GCP["GCP (child)"]

Matching behavior:

Job requires Candidate has Match? Why Cloud AWS Yes AWS is a child of Cloud AWS Cloud Yes Cloud is the parent of AWS AWS Azure No Siblings are NOT matched AWS AWS Yes Exact match

Why no sibling matching: AWS and Azure are different technologies. A candidate knowing AWS doesn't mean they know Azure. The parent-child relationship captures "specific implies general" and "general covers specific" — but not "specific implies other specific."

This matching is applied in app/api/match.py:64 when building the SPEA2 matrix:

for s, _ in job_skills_list:
    related_names = resolver.get_related_skills(s.name)
    related_ids = {s.id}
    for rname in related_names:
        rs = store.get_skill_by_normalized(rname.lower())
        if rs:
            related_ids.add(rs.id)
    job_skill_related_ids[idx] = related_ids

docs.title: JD Skill Normalization
docs.text:
Source: app/services/jd_extraction.py:198 — normalize_jd_skills()

After the LLM extracts skills from a job description, three post-processing steps run:

docs.title: 1. Compound Decomposition
docs.text:
_decompose_compound_skill(name) at line 138:

Splits multi-skill phrases using connectors ("or", "and", "/"):

Input Output "JavaScript or TypeScript" ["JavaScript", "TypeScript"] "Python and Django" ["Python", "Django"] "Async processing or task queues" ["Asynchronous Processing", "Task Queues"] "Automated testing for backend and frontend" ["Automated Testing"] (strips prepositional clause first)

Only splits if resulting parts are ≤ 4 words each (avoids splitting sentences).

docs.title: 2. Qualifier Stripping
docs.text:
_strip_qualifiers(name) at line 159:

Removes noise prefixes and suffixes that prevent taxonomy matching:

Input Output "CI/CD pipelines" "CI/CD" "Cloud deployment workflows" "Cloud" "Modern JavaScript" "JavaScript" "Advanced Python programming" "Python"

Iterates up to 5 times until stable (handles "Cloud deployment workflows" → "Cloud deployment" → "Cloud").

Stripped suffixes: pipelines, workflows, services, tools, frameworks, platforms, design, development, implementation, management, systems, testing, processing, orchestration, databases, etc.

Stripped prefixes: Modern, Advanced, Basic, Core, Enterprise, Production, Strong, Solid, Deep, Hands-on, Expert, etc.

docs.title: 3. Taxonomy Resolution + Deduplication
docs.text: For each skill: 1. Try taxonomy resolution (exact → fuzzy) 2. If matched as "esco": use canonical name 3. If "other" (open vocabulary): try stripping qualifiers and re-resolving 4. If still "other": keep as-is 5. Skip duplicates (case-insensitive, preserving order)

docs.title: SkillNormalizer Wrapper
docs.text:
Source: app/services/skill_library.py

Thin wrapper around TaxonomyResolver. Used throughout the codebase for backward compatibility. The normalize(name) method calls resolver.resolve(name) and returns the canonical name.

docs.title: Extending the Taxonomy
docs.text:
To add new skills to data/taxonomy/esco_skills.json:

  1. Add a new object to the JSON array: 

    {
  "id": "custom_001",
  "preferred_label": "LangChain",
  "alt_labels": ["Langchain", "lang chain"],
  "parent": null
}

  2. To make it a child of an existing skill, set parent to the parent's id: 

    {
  "id": "custom_002",
  "preferred_label": "Amazon Bedrock",
  "alt_labels": ["Bedrock"],
  "parent": "skill_aws_id"
}

  3. Restart the backend (taxonomy is loaded at startup)

Requirements: - id must be unique - preferred_label is the canonical name used everywhere - alt_labels are optional but improve fuzzy matching - parent must reference an existing id or be null

docs.title: Validation Metrics Reference
docs.text: Mathematical definitions and interpretation of all statistics computed by _compute_validation_stats() in app/api/validation.py:61.

docs.title: Historical validation
docs.text:
• Positive set: Candidates marked as "interviewed" or "hired" via PUT /jobs/{job_id}/candidates/{cid}/status
  • Top-K determination: K = count of interviewed candidates. If zero, K = total ranked candidates.

docs.title: Supervised validation
docs.text:
• Positive set: Candidate IDs in recruiter_picks (set via POST /validation/{id}/recruiter-picks)
  • Top-K determination: K = recruiter_pick_count (count of recruiter picks). If zero, K = total ranked candidates.

docs.title: Sets
docs.text:
model_top_k         = set of first K candidate IDs in SPEA2 ranking
ground_truth_positives = set of positive candidate IDs (interviewed/hired or recruiter picks)

TP (True Positives)  = model_top_k ∩ ground_truth_positives
FP (False Positives) = model_top_k - ground_truth_positives
FN (False Negatives) = ground_truth_positives - model_top_k

docs.title: Precision
docs.text:
Precision = TP / (TP + FP)

"Of the candidates VICA selected in its top-K, what fraction are actual positives?"

High precision = VICA's picks are reliable. Low precision = VICA recommends too many wrong candidates.

docs.title: Recall
docs.text:
Recall = TP / (TP + FN)

"Of the actual positive candidates, what fraction did VICA place in its top-K?"

High recall = VICA finds most of the right candidates. Low recall = VICA misses candidates you care about.

docs.title: F1 Score
docs.text:
F1 = 2 × Precision × Recall / (Precision + Recall)

Harmonic mean of precision and recall. Ranges from 0 to 1. F1 = 0 when either precision or recall is 0. F1 = 1 when both are perfect.

docs.title: Accuracy@K
docs.text:
Accuracy@K = TP / K

"What fraction of VICA's top-K slots contain actual positives?" Equivalent to precision when K equals the model's top-K size.

docs.title: Rank Analysis
docs.text:
For each ground-truth positive candidate, find their position in VICA's full ranking (1-indexed).

Metric Definition avg_rank Mean rank position of ground-truth positives median_rank Median rank position best_rank Lowest (best) rank among positives worst_rank Highest (worst) rank among positives not_ranked Count of positives not in the ranking at all positions Sorted list of all rank positions

Interpretation: If ground-truth candidates cluster near the top (low rank numbers), VICA's ordering agrees with reality. If avg_rank is much larger than K, VICA is placing the right candidates too low.

docs.title: Skill Coverage
docs.text:
Per-skill analysis: how many candidates in the pool have each job skill.

{
  "skill": "Python",
  "candidates_with_skill": 18,
  "total_candidates": 50,
  "coverage_pct": 36.0
}

Interpretation: - Skills with very high coverage (>80%) are not discriminating — almost everyone has them - Skills with very low coverage (<10%) might be too niche or poorly matched by the taxonomy - Useful for identifying which skills actually differentiate candidates in the pool

docs.title: Fitness Distribution
docs.text: Statistical summary of SPEA2 fitness scores across all candidates.

docs.title: Ground-truth positives vs non-positives
docs.text:
{
  "ground_truth_fitness": {"mean": 4.8000, "median": 5.0000},
  "non_ground_truth_fitness": {"mean": 2.9000, "median": 2.7000}
}

Interpretation: If ground-truth candidates have significantly higher mean fitness than non-positives, VICA's scoring function is working well — it assigns higher scores to the "right" candidates even if the top-K cutoff doesn't capture all of them.

docs.title: Historical-Specific Metrics
docs.text:
Only computed for historical validation:

Metric Definition interviewed_count Total candidates marked as interviewed hired_count Total candidates marked as hired hired_in_top_k Count of hired candidates that appear in VICA's top-K

docs.title: Aggregate Report
docs.text:
Computed across all completed validation runs (GET /validation/aggregate/report).

{
  "total_runs": 5,
  "historical_runs": 3,
  "supervised_runs": 2,
  "avg_precision": 0.7200,
  "avg_recall": 0.6500,
  "avg_f1": 0.6833,
  "avg_accuracy_at_k": 0.7000,
  "std_precision": 0.0800,
  "std_recall": 0.1200,
  "std_f1": 0.0950,
  "total_candidates_evaluated": 250,
  "total_ground_truth": 40,
  "runs": [...]
}

Standard deviation is only computed when there are 2+ runs. Low std = consistent performance. High std = variable results across runs (may indicate sensitivity to skill configuration).

docs.title: Full Stats Object Schema
docs.text:
Returned by POST /validation/{id}/compute-stats:

{
  "run_type": "historical",
  "candidate_count": 50,
  "required_skills": ["Python", "AWS"],
  "preferred_skills": ["Docker", "Redis"],
  "total_skills": 4,
  "top_k": 5,
  "ground_truth_count": 5,
  "model_top_k_count": 5,
  "true_positives": 3,
  "false_positives": 2,
  "false_negatives": 2,
  "precision": 0.6000,
  "recall": 0.6000,
  "f1_score": 0.6000,
  "accuracy_at_k": 0.6000,
  "rank_analysis": {
    "avg_rank": 4.2,
    "median_rank": 3,
    "best_rank": 1,

  },

docs.title: Developer Guide
docs.text: Technical reference for building, extending, and deploying VICA.

docs.title: System Architecture
docs.text:
graph TB
    subgraph FE["Frontend"]
        REACT["React 18 + TypeScript<br/>Vite / Nginx :3000"]
    end

    subgraph BE["Backend (FastAPI :8000)"]
        direction TB
        R_CAND["candidates.py"]
        R_JOBS["jobs.py"]
        R_MATCH["match.py"]
        R_REPORT["report.py"]
        R_VAL["validation.py"]

        SVC["Services Layer"]
        STORE["In-Memory Store<br/>+ JSON Persistence"]
    end

    subgraph GPU["GPU Services (Single GPU)"]
        VLLM["vLLM Container<br/>Qwen2.5-VL-3B :8001"]
        OLLAMA["Ollama Server<br/>llama3.1:8b :11434"]
    end

    subgraph DATA["Data Layer"]
        FILES["data/files/<br/>CV storage"]
        DB["data/db/<br/>JSON persistence"]

    end

    REACT -->|"/api"| R_CAND & R_JOBS & R_MATCH & R_REPORT & R_VAL

docs.title: Data Flow: Full Pipeline
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as Recruiter
    participant FE as Frontend
    participant API as Backend API
    participant GPU as GPU Orchestrator
    participant OCR as vLLM (OCR)
    participant LLM as Ollama (LLM)
    participant DB as Store

    U->>FE: Upload CVs
    FE->>API: POST /candidates/upload
    API->>DB: Store file + record

    U->>FE: Parse All
    FE->>API: POST /candidates/{id}/preprocess
    API->>GPU: ensure_ocr()
    GPU->>LLM: Unload model
    GPU->>OCR: Start container
    API->>OCR: Extract text (per page)
    API->>DB: Save agenderized text

    U->>FE: Extract Skills
    FE->>API: POST /candidates/{id}/extract-skills
    API->>GPU: ensure_llm()
    GPU->>OCR: Stop container
    GPU->>LLM: Load model
    API->>LLM: Deep skill extraction
    API->>DB: Save skills + evidence

    U->>FE: Run Match
    FE->>API: POST /match/run-and-analyze (SSE)
    API->>DB: Build skill matrix
    API->>API: SPEA2 ranking
    API->>GPU: ensure_llm()
    loop For each top-K candidate
        API->>LLM: Semantic analysis
        API-->>FE: SSE progress event
    end
    API->>DB: Save results
    API-->>FE: SSE done event

docs.title: POST /match/run
docs.text:
Run SPEA2 ranking only (no semantic analysis).

Request body: 

{
  "job_id": 1,
  "archive_size": 10,
  "include_global": false
}

Response: 

{
  "match_run_id": 1,
  "archive_size": 10,
  "results": [
    {"candidate_id": 5, "rank": 1, "fitness": 4.250, "r_stat": 0.0},
    {"candidate_id": 12, "rank": 2, "fitness": 3.800, "r_stat": 2.0}
  ],
  "remaining": [
    {"candidate_id": 8, "rank": 11, "fitness": 2.100, "r_stat": 5.0},
    {"candidate_id": 3, "rank": 12, "fitness": 1.500, "r_stat": 8.0}
  ]
}

  • results: Top-K candidates (archive), ranked by fitness. These receive full LLM analysis and reports.
  • remaining: All other candidates not in the top-K, ranked by SPEA2 R statistic (ascending). No reports generated for these.

docs.title: POST /match/run-and-analyze
docs.text:
Run SPEA2 + stream LLM analysis progress via SSE (Server-Sent Events).

Request body: Same as /match/run.

Response: text/event-stream with events:

Event Data When progress {"phase": "ranking", "current": 0, "total": 1, "message": "..."} Start of ranking ranking_done Same as /match/run response SPEA2 complete progress {"phase": "gpu", ...} GPU switching progress {"phase": "analysis", "current": 0, "total": 5, "message": "Analyzing candidate 1/5..."} Per-candidate done {...run_result, "analysis": {"analyzed": 5}} All complete

docs.title: Data Flow: Regular Loop
docs.text:
graph TD
    A["1. Upload CV<br/>POST /candidates/upload"] -->|"Store file, create record"| B["2. Parse CV<br/>POST /candidates/{id}/preprocess"]
    B -->|"GPU → OCR, extract text, agenderize"| C["3. Extract Skills<br/>POST /candidates/{id}/extract-skills"]
    C -->|"GPU → LLM, deep extraction + taxonomy"| D["4. Create Job<br/>POST /jobs/"]
    D -->|"Store raw JD text"| E["5. Extract JD Skills<br/>GET /jobs/extract-skills"]
    E -->|"GPU → LLM, decompose + normalize"| F["6. Validate Skills<br/>PUT /jobs/{id}/skills"]
    F -->|"Recruiter reorders, toggles required"| G["7. Run Match<br/>POST /match/run-and-analyze (SSE)"]
    G -->|"SPEA2 ranking + LLM analysis"| H["8. View Report<br/>GET /report/cv/{id}/{job_id}"]

docs.title: Data Flow: Validation Loop
docs.text:
Same as regular loop with these additions:

graph TD
    A["1. Create Validation Run<br/>POST /validation"] -->|"historical or supervised"| B["2. Setup<br/>Upload CVs → Parse → Extract → Set JD → Validate"]
    B --> C{"Validation Type?"}
    C -->|Historical| D["3a. Mark Status<br/>interviewed / hired / rejected"]
    C -->|Supervised| E["3b. Set Recruiter Picks<br/>POST /validation/{id}/recruiter-picks"]
    D --> F["4. Run Match<br/>SPEA2 + LLM Analysis (SSE)"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["5. Compute Stats<br/>Precision, Recall, F1, Accuracy@K"]
    G --> H["6. Stats Report<br/>HTML per-run report"]
    H --> I["7. Aggregate Report<br/>HTML across all runs"]

docs.title: API Layer
docs.text:
Five routers mounted in app/main.py:

Router Prefix File Responsibility Candidates /candidates app/api/candidates.py Upload, parse, extract skills Jobs /jobs app/api/jobs.py CRUD, JD parsing, skill validation, candidate association Match /match app/api/match.py SPEA2 ranking, semantic analysis, SSE streaming Report /report app/api/report.py HTML report generation Validation /validation app/api/validation.py Validation runs, stats, recruiter picks

Root endpoints in app/main.py: /health, /gpu-status, /gpu/ensure-ocr, /gpu/ensure-llm, /gpu/release, /llm-status.

See API Reference for complete endpoint documentation.

docs.title: Frontend Structure
docs.text:
frontend/src/
├── main.tsx               # React entry point
├── App.tsx                # Router: jobs | job-detail | global-candidates | validation | settings
├── Sidebar.tsx            # Navigation sidebar with quick actions, overview stats, settings link
├── JobList.tsx            # List all jobs with search bar, filters (status), and sorting
├── JobDetail.tsx          # 3-tab view: Candidates, Description & Skills, Match Results
│   ├── CandidatePool      # (embedded) Upload, parse, extract, status management
│   ├── JobSkillsEditor    # (embedded) Drag-drop skill ordering, required/preferred
│   └── MatchRun           # (embedded) Run SPEA2, view results + inline reports
├── GlobalCandidates.tsx   # All candidates across all jobs
├── ValidationRunList.tsx  # List validation runs with search bar, filters (type, status), and sorting
├── ValidationRunDetail.tsx # Full validation workflow (both historical + supervised)
├── Settings.tsx           # Settings page: GPU management, LLM/OCR config, about
└── CVManager.tsx          # Standalone candidate management

Key patterns: - State management via useState + useCallback (no external state library) - SSE streaming for match progress (EventSource API) - Drag-and-drop via @dnd-kit (core + sortable) - All API calls go through /api proxy (Vite dev or Nginx production) - Search/filter/sort on list pages via useMemo for client-side filtering

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
VICA uses an in-memory singleton store with JSON file persistence. No external database is required.

  • Speed: All reads are dict lookups (O(1))
  • Durability: Every mutation writes through to data/db/*.json via atomic temp-file-then-rename
  • Thread safety: ID counter uses threading.Lock; GPU orchestrator uses asyncio.Lock
  • Source: app/store.py

docs.title: Skill
docs.text:
@dataclass
class Skill:
    id: int
    name: str                # Original name as extracted
    normalized_name: str     # Lowercased canonical (used for dedup)
    source: str = "tmc"      # "esco" (taxonomy match) or "other" (open vocabulary)

docs.title: Candidate
docs.text:
@dataclass
class Candidate:
    id: int
    external_id: str | None        # UUID assigned at upload
    cv_storage_key: str             # Path in data/files/ (e.g., "cvs/{uuid}_{filename}")
    cv_filename: str | None         # Original filename
    anonymized_text: str | None     # Agenderized + sectioned CV text (after preprocess)
    evidence_index: str | None      # JSON: {skill_name: [quote1, quote2, ...]}
    temporal_features: str | None   # JSON: {years_of_experience: 8.0, ...}
    deep_skills: str | None         # JSON: [{name, proficiency, evidence, context, justification}, ...]
    match_analysis: str | None      # JSON: {skill_assessments, transferable_skills, interview_questions, summary}
    extraction_meta: str | None     # JSON: {extraction_method, model, skills_found, llm_enabled}
    analysis_meta: str | None       # JSON: {analysis_method, model}

docs.title: JobDescription
docs.text:
@dataclass
class JobDescription:
    id: int
    external_id: str | None
    title: str | None
    raw_text: str                   # Full JD text
    created_at: str | None          # ISO 8601 UTC timestamp
    requirements: str | None        # JSON: {min_years_experience, education_level, education_field}

docs.title: JobSkill
docs.text:
@dataclass
class JobSkill:
    job_id: int
    skill_id: int
    position: int          # Recruiter-ordered position (0 = most important)
    required: bool = True  # True = required, False = preferred

docs.title: MatchRun
docs.text:
@dataclass
class MatchRun:
    id: int
    job_id: int
    archive_size: int
    results: List[Tuple[int, float, float]]  # [(candidate_id, fitness, r_stat), ...]
    remaining: List[Tuple[int, float, float]] | None  # Non-top-K candidates [(cand_id, fitness, r_stat), ...]
    created_at: str | None

docs.title: ValidationRun
docs.text:
@dataclass
class ValidationRun:
    id: int
    title: str
    run_type: str           # "historical" or "supervised"
    status: str             # "setup" → "candidates_ready" → "jd_ready" → "processing" → "matched" → "completed"
    created_at: str | None
    job_id: int | None              # Internal job created for this validation
    interviewed_count: int | None   # Historical: count of interviewed candidates
    recruiter_pick_count: int | None # Supervised: count of recruiter picks
    recruiter_picks: str | None     # JSON: [candidate_id, ...]
    stats: str | None               # JSON: full validation statistics blob
    match_run_id: int | None        # Reference to SPEA2 results

docs.title: Relationships
docs.text:
erDiagram
    Skill ||--o{ candidate_skill : "has"
    Candidate ||--o{ candidate_skill : "has"
    Skill ||--o{ JobSkill : "listed in"
    JobDescription ||--o{ JobSkill : "requires"
    Candidate }o--o{ JobDescription : "job_candidates"
    JobDescription ||--o{ MatchRun : "produces"
    ValidationRun ||--|| JobDescription : "internal job"
    ValidationRun ||--o| MatchRun : "references"

    Skill {
        int id
        string name
        string normalized_name
        string source
    }

    Candidate {
        int id
        string external_id
        string cv_storage_key
        string anonymized_text
        json deep_skills
        json match_analysis
    }

    JobDescription {
        int id
        string title
        string raw_text
        string created_at
        json requirements
    }

    JobSkill {
        int job_id
        int skill_id
        int position
        bool required
    }

    MatchRun {
        int id
        int job_id
        int archive_size
        json results
        json remaining
    }

    ValidationRun {
        int id
        string title
        string run_type
        string status
        json stats
    }

candidate_skill: Set[Tuple[int, int]] — sparse representation. Only stores (candidate_id, skill_id) pairs for skills the candidate has.

job_candidates: Dict[int, List[int]] — job_id → list of candidate_ids.

job_skill: List[JobSkill] — ordered list; position determines weight in SPEA2.

docs.title: File Storage
docs.text:
Directory: data/files/

Key format: cvs/{uuid}_{original_filename}

Interface: app/storage/base.py — LocalStorage class with upload(), download(), exists() methods.

docs.title: Persistence Files
docs.text:
All in data/db/:

File Contents Format counter.json {"next_id": N} Auto-increment counter skills.json {"1": {id, name, normalized_name, source}, ...} ID → Skill candidates.json {"1": {id, external_id, cv_storage_key, ...}, ...} ID → Candidate candidate_skill.json {"1": [3, 5, 12], "2": [1, 5]} candidate_id → [skill_ids] jobs.json {"1": {id, title, raw_text, created_at}, ...} ID → Job job_skill.json [{job_id, skill_id, position, required}, ...] Ordered list job_candidates.json {"1": [1, 2, 3], "2": [4, 5]} job_id → [candidate_ids] match_runs.json {"1": {id, job_id, archive_size, results, created_at}} ID → MatchRun candidate_status.json {"1": {"5": {"status": "hired", "updated_at": "..."}}} job_id → cand_id → status validation_runs.json {"1": {id, title, run_type, status, ...}} ID → ValidationRun

Write strategy: Atomic writes via _atomic_write() — write to .tmp file, then os.replace() to target path. This prevents corruption if the process crashes mid-write.

docs.title: Singleton Access
docs.text:
from app.store import get_store

store = get_store()  # Returns the singleton Store instance

reset_store() clears all in-memory data and rewrites all JSON files with empty content. Used by test scripts.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Python 3.11+
  • Node.js 18+ (for frontend)
  • Ollama (for LLM) — https://ollama.com
  • NVIDIA GPU + Docker (for OCR) — optional, falls back to pdfplumber

docs.title: Backend
docs.text:
cd vica
python -m venv .venv
.venv/Scripts/activate        # Windows
# source .venv/bin/activate   # Linux/macOS
pip install -r requirements.txt

# Pull the LLM model
ollama pull llama3.1:8b
ollama serve                   # keep running in separate terminal

# Start backend
python run_backend.py          # or: uvicorn app.main:app --reload --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000

docs.title: Frontend
docs.text:
cd vica/frontend
npm install
npm run dev                    # Vite dev server on http://localhost:5173

Vite proxies /api → http://localhost:8000.

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text:
Copy .env.example to .env (optional — defaults work for local dev):

LLM_BASE_URL=http://localhost:11434/v1
LLM_MODEL=llama3.1:8b
LLM_ENABLED=true
OCR_BASE_URL=http://localhost:8001
OCR_ENABLED=true
SECRET_KEY=change-me
DEBUG=true
DOCKER_NETWORK=vica-network

docs.title: Docker Compose (Full GPU)
docs.text: Full stack with automatic GPU orchestration. Requires NVIDIA GPU + Docker with GPU support.

docs.title: Services
docs.text: Service Container Port Purpose Backend vica-backend 8000 FastAPI server Frontend vica-frontend 3000 React app via Nginx Ollama vica-ollama 11434 LLM server (always running) OCR vica-ocr 8001 vLLM OCR (managed by backend, not auto-started)

docs.title: First-time Setup
docs.text:
cd vica

# Start services
docker compose up -d --build

# Pull the LLM model into the Ollama container
docker exec vica-ollama ollama pull llama3.1:8b
# Or use the setup script:
# bash scripts/setup-ollama-model.sh

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
docker compose up -d              # Start
docker compose up -d --build      # Rebuild and start
docker compose logs -f backend    # Watch backend logs
docker compose down               # Stop everything

docs.title: Key Configuration
docs.text:
The OCR container (vica-ocr) is defined in compose but not auto-started — it uses the ocr-manual profile. The backend starts/stops it dynamically via the Docker socket when OCR is needed.

# Backend needs Docker socket to manage OCR container
volumes:
  - /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock

Environment overrides inside Docker: - LLM_BASE_URL=http://ollama:11434/v1 (Docker service name, not localhost) - OCR_BASE_URL=http://host.docker.internal:8001 (OCR container exposes port to host)

docs.title: Volumes
docs.text: Volume Purpose ./data Persistent data (CVs, JSON DB, reports, taxonomy) vica-ollama-models Ollama downloaded models vica-vllm-cache vLLM model cache

docs.title: Docker Compose (Portable / CPU-only)
docs.text:
No GPU, no Ollama, no vLLM. Uses deterministic fallbacks: - OCR → pdfplumber (text-based PDF extraction only) - Skill extraction → phrase matching (taxonomy keyword matching) - Analysis → binary presence mapping (no semantic analysis)

Use case: Viewing past run results on a laptop, lightweight testing, demo environments.

docker compose -f docker-compose.portable.yml up -d --build
docker compose -f docker-compose.portable.yml down

Only two services: backend (port 8000) + frontend (port 3000).

Environment: LLM_ENABLED=false, OCR_ENABLED=false.

docs.title: Data Directory
docs.text:
All persistent data lives in data/:

data/
├── db/              # JSON persistence (candidates, jobs, skills, matches, etc.)
├── files/           # Uploaded CV files
│   └── cvs/         # CVs stored as {uuid}_{filename}
├── taxonomy/        # ESCO skills taxonomy (esco_skills.json)
├── skill_library/   # Cached skill phrases
└── reports/         # Generated HTML reports (if saved)

Portability: Copy the entire data/ directory to move between machines. The portable Docker setup reads the same data/ structure.

docs.title: Nginx Configuration
docs.text:
The frontend Dockerfile builds the React app and serves it via Nginx. nginx.conf proxies /api requests to the backend:

location /api/ {
    proxy_pass http://backend:8000/;
}

In production, the frontend is served on port 3000 via Nginx. In development, Vite's built-in proxy handles the same routing.

docs.title: Test Run
docs.text:
Verify the full pipeline works:

# Without LLM (phrase matching, fast)
python scripts/run_test.py

# With LLM (requires Ollama running)
python scripts/run_test.py --llm

Seeds 100 fake CVs + 1 tech job, runs SPEA2, generates a report at data/test_report.html.

docs.title: Problem
docs.text:
VICA uses two GPU-intensive models: - OCR: Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct via vLLM (reads text from PDF/image files) - LLM: llama3.1:8b via Ollama (extracts skills, analyzes candidates)

On a single-GPU setup (e.g., RTX 5070), both models cannot be loaded into VRAM simultaneously. The GPU orchestrator manages exclusive access.

Source: app/services/gpu_orchestrator.py

docs.title: Three Modes
docs.text:
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> IDLE
    IDLE --> OCR: ensure_ocr()
    IDLE --> LLM: ensure_llm()
    OCR --> LLM: ensure_llm()
    LLM --> OCR: ensure_ocr()
    OCR --> IDLE: release_gpu()
    LLM --> IDLE: release_gpu()

    state OCR {
        [*] --> vLLM_Running
        vLLM_Running: vLLM container active
        vLLM_Running: Ollama model unloaded
    }

    state LLM {
        [*] --> Ollama_Loaded
        Ollama_Loaded: Ollama model in VRAM
        Ollama_Loaded: vLLM container stopped
    }

    state IDLE {
        [*] --> No_GPU
        No_GPU: Neither service using GPU
    }
class GpuMode(str, Enum):
    OCR = "ocr"    # vLLM container running, Ollama model unloaded
    LLM = "llm"    # Ollama model loaded, vLLM container stopped
    IDLE = "idle"   # Neither service using GPU

docs.title: OCR Service (vLLM)
docs.text:
Container name: vica-ocr

Image: vllm/vllm-openai:latest

Model: Qwen/Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct

Configuration: - GPU memory utilization: 85% - Served model name: qwen2.5-vl - Max model length: 16,384 tokens - Image limit: 1 per prompt

Lifecycle: 1. First run: Backend creates the Docker container via docker run. Downloads model (~6GB). Timeout: 300s. 2. Subsequent runs: Backend restarts the existing stopped container via docker start. Timeout: 120s. 3. Stop: Backend stops the container via docker stop (container persists for fast restart).

Health check: GET /health or GET /v1/models on the OCR base URL.

docs.title: LLM Service (Ollama)
docs.text:
Server: Ollama (native or Docker), always running.

Model: Configured via LLM_MODEL env var (default: llama3.1:8b).

Load/unload: Ollama keeps models in VRAM until explicitly unloaded. - Load: POST /api/generate with a small prompt triggers model loading into VRAM - Unload: POST /api/generate with keep_alive: 0 releases VRAM

Health checks: - Server alive: GET /api/tags returns 200 - Model loaded in VRAM: GET /api/ps shows the configured model

docs.title: ensure_ocr()
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant API as API Request
    participant GPU as GPU Orchestrator
    participant OLL as Ollama
    participant VLLM as vLLM Container

    API->>GPU: ensure_ocr()
    alt OCR already healthy
        GPU-->>API: return True (fast path)
    else Switch needed
        GPU->>GPU: Acquire asyncio lock
        GPU->>OLL: POST /api/generate (keep_alive=0)
        Note over OLL: Unload model from VRAM
        GPU->>GPU: Wait 2s (VRAM release)
        GPU->>VLLM: docker start / docker run
        loop Poll health (120s / 300s first-time)
            GPU->>VLLM: GET /health
        end
        alt Healthy
            GPU->>GPU: Set mode = OCR
            GPU-->>API: return True
        else Failed
            GPU->>VLLM: docker stop
            GPU-->>API: return False
        end
    end

docs.title: ensure_llm()
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant API as API Request
    participant GPU as GPU Orchestrator
    participant VLLM as vLLM Container
    participant OLL as Ollama

    API->>GPU: ensure_llm()
    alt Ollama model already loaded
        GPU-->>API: return True (fast path)
    else Switch needed
        GPU->>GPU: Acquire asyncio lock
        GPU->>VLLM: docker stop
        GPU->>GPU: Wait 3s (VRAM release)
        GPU->>OLL: Wait for server (30s)
        GPU->>OLL: POST /api/generate (preload)
        Note over OLL: Load model into VRAM (120s)
        alt Success
            GPU->>GPU: Set mode = LLM
            GPU-->>API: return True
        else Failed
            GPU-->>API: return False
        end
    end

docs.title: release_gpu()
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant API as API Request
    participant GPU as GPU Orchestrator
    participant OLL as Ollama
    participant VLLM as vLLM Container

    API->>GPU: release_gpu()
    GPU->>GPU: Acquire asyncio lock
    GPU->>OLL: Unload model
    GPU->>VLLM: docker stop
    GPU->>GPU: Set mode = IDLE
    GPU-->>API: done

docs.title: Thread Safety
docs.text: All switching operations go through _gpu_lock = asyncio.Lock(). This serializes concurrent API requests that need different GPU modes — the first request switches, subsequent requests wait and find the GPU already in the right mode.

docs.title: Auto-Triggering
docs.text:
API endpoints automatically call the orchestrator before GPU-dependent operations:

Endpoint Triggers GPU Mode POST /candidates/{id}/preprocess PDF/image files need OCR OCR POST /candidates/{id}/extract-skills LLM skill extraction LLM POST /jobs/parse-document PDF/image JD parsing OCR GET /jobs/extract-skills LLM JD skill extraction LLM POST /match/run-and-analyze LLM semantic analysis LLM POST /match/analyze/{id} LLM semantic analysis LLM POST /validation/{id}/run-match LLM semantic analysis LLM

If the GPU is already in the right mode, the fast path returns immediately (no switch).

docs.title: Manual Control
docs.text:
API endpoints for direct GPU management:

Endpoint Action GET /gpu-status Return current mode + health of each service POST /gpu/ensure-ocr Force switch to OCR mode POST /gpu/ensure-llm Force switch to LLM mode POST /gpu/release Release GPU from all services

docs.title: Docker Socket Requirement
docs.text:
The backend needs access to the Docker socket to manage the vLLM container:

# docker-compose.yml
volumes:
  - /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock

Without this, the backend cannot start/stop the OCR container and will log errors. OCR-dependent operations will fall back to pdfplumber (text-only PDF parsing, no image support).

docs.title: Timeouts
docs.text: Operation Timeout Reason vLLM first start 300s Downloads model (~6GB) vLLM restart 120s Model already cached Ollama server reachable 30s Wait for server startup Ollama model preload 120s Load model into VRAM Docker stop 30s Graceful container shutdown Health check poll 5s per check Individual HTTP request Health poll interval 3s (OCR), 2s (Ollama) Between poll attempts

docs.title: For Recruiters
docs.text: Welcome to VICA. This section covers everything you need to screen candidates, interpret reports, and validate VICA's accuracy.

docs.title: How VICA Works (30-Second Version)
docs.text:
graph LR
    A["You upload CVs<br/>+ Job Description"] --> B["VICA reads<br/>every CV"]
    B --> C["AI identifies<br/>skills + evidence"]
    C --> D["Algorithm ranks<br/>candidates"]
    D --> E["You review reports<br/>with citations"]

VICA does not make hiring decisions

It provides a ranked shortlist with evidence. The final decision is always yours.

docs.title: Guides
docs.text: Guide What it covers Hiring Guide Step-by-step: upload CVs, set job skills, run match, read reports Validation Guide Measure VICA's accuracy against your past hiring or your own expert picks Understanding Reports How to read recommendation badges, skill tables, citations, and interview questions Glossary Plain-language definitions for every VICA term

docs.title: Quick Reference: Recommendation Badges
docs.text: Badge Meaning Action Strong Yes Excellent fit across required skills Interview — high priority Yes Good fit, meets most requirements Interview — standard priority Maybe Mixed signals, gaps or unverified claims Review carefully No Missing critical required skills Deprioritize

docs.title: Quick Reference: Match Levels
docs.text: Level Meaning Badge Color Strong match Direct experience with real projects  Green Partial match Some evidence but limited depth  Yellow Transferable Related skill that could substitute  Blue No match No evidence found  Red

docs.title: Glossary
docs.text: Plain-language definitions for terms you'll encounter in the VICA interface and reports.

docs.title: Required Skill
docs.text: A skill that the candidate must have for the job. Required skills carry more weight in the ranking (2x compared to preferred skills). In the UI, these have green borders and checked boxes.

docs.title: Preferred Skill
docs.text: A "nice to have" skill. Candidates with preferred skills rank higher, but missing them is not a disqualifier. In the UI, these have gray borders and unchecked boxes.

docs.title: Proficiency Levels
docs.text:
How deeply a candidate knows a skill, based on evidence in their CV:

Level Meaning Expert Years of hands-on experience, led projects using this skill Proficient Used in real projects with clear practical experience Familiar Some exposure — coursework, minor usage, or side projects Mentioned Listed on the CV but no supporting evidence

docs.title: Match Levels
docs.text:
How well a candidate matches a specific job skill:

Level Meaning Strong match Clear direct experience — real projects and evidence Partial match Some evidence but limited depth, or a related but not exact skill Transferable Has a closely related skill that could substitute No match No evidence of this skill or anything related

docs.title: Recommendation
docs.text:
The overall assessment for a candidate:

Badge Meaning Strong Yes Excellent fit — strong evidence across required skills Yes Good fit — meets most requirements Maybe Mixed — some gaps or unverified claims No Not suitable — missing critical skills

docs.title: Fitness Score
docs.text: A number showing how well a candidate's skills match the job, considering skill importance and required/preferred classification. Higher = better match. This is not a rating "out of 10" — it's a weighted sum that depends on how many skills the job has.

docs.title: SPEA2
docs.text: The algorithm that ranks candidates. It considers all job skills simultaneously and finds candidates that are "not outperformed" by others across the skill set. You don't need to understand how it works to use VICA — just know that it produces the ranked shortlist. For technical details, see the SPEA2 Algorithm doc.

docs.title: ESCO Taxonomy
docs.text: A standard European vocabulary of professional skills. VICA uses it to recognize when different names refer to the same skill (e.g., "Amazon Web Services" and "AWS" are the same thing). Skills not in the taxonomy are still kept and used.

docs.title: Open Vocabulary
docs.text: Skills that VICA doesn't recognize from the standard taxonomy. These are kept as-is and still used for matching. Example: a very specific internal tool name.

docs.title: OCR
docs.text: "Optical Character Recognition" — the technology that reads text from images or scanned PDFs. VICA uses a vision AI model (Qwen2.5-VL) for this. If OCR isn't available, VICA falls back to basic PDF text extraction.

docs.title: LLM
docs.text: "Large Language Model" — the AI model that reads CVs, extracts skills, and writes candidate analyses. VICA runs this locally (not in the cloud) using Ollama. If the LLM isn't available, VICA falls back to keyword matching.

docs.title: GPU
docs.text: The graphics processor used to run the AI models. VICA manages a single GPU shared between OCR and LLM tasks, switching automatically as needed.

docs.title: Validation Run
docs.text: A test to measure how well VICA performs. You compare VICA's ranking against known outcomes (historical) or your own judgment (supervised).

docs.title: Ground Truth
docs.text: The "correct answer" used to evaluate VICA. In historical validation, this is which candidates were actually interviewed/hired. In supervised validation, this is which candidates you independently picked.

docs.title: Precision
docs.text: "Of the candidates VICA picked, how many were actually good?" Higher = fewer false recommendations.

docs.title: Recall
docs.text: "Of the candidates that should have been picked, how many did VICA find?" Higher = fewer missed candidates.

docs.title: F1 Score
docs.text: A combined measure of precision and recall. Ranges from 0% (terrible) to 100% (perfect). Generally, above 70% is good.

docs.title: Accuracy@K
docs.text: "What fraction of VICA's top-K picks are actual positives?" Similar to precision, focused specifically on the shortlist size.

docs.title: What is VICA?
docs.text: VICA is a tool that helps you screen candidates for a job opening. You upload CVs and a job description, and VICA reads each CV, identifies skills, and ranks candidates based on how well their skills match the job requirements. It does not make hiring decisions — it gives you a ranked shortlist with evidence to support your review.

docs.title: Before You Start
docs.text:
• The system should be running (you'll see the VICA interface at http://localhost:3000 or your configured URL)
  • Have your job description ready (text you can paste, or a PDF/image file)
  • Have your CV files ready (PDF, DOCX, or image files)
  • Check the GPU status badge in the sidebar footer — it shows whether the AI services are available
  • For detailed GPU and system configuration, click Settings at the bottom of the sidebar

docs.title: Step 1: Create a Job
docs.text:
1. From the left sidebar, click Jobs (or use the New Job quick action in the sidebar)
  2. Click "New Job" in the page header to open the creation form
  3. You have two options:
  4. Paste text: Copy your job description into the text area
  5. Upload PDF/image: Click "Upload JD" to upload a PDF or image of the JD — the system will extract the text using OCR
  6. Give the job a title (e.g., "Senior Backend Engineer")
  7. Click "Create Job"
  8. A green badge confirms the job was created

docs.title: Step 2: Upload CVs
docs.text:
1. Navigate to the job detail view (click on the job you just created)
  2. Go to the Candidates tab
  3. Click "Upload CVs" — you can select multiple files at once
  4. Each uploaded file appears in the table with a gray "Uploaded" badge
  5. The progress bar shows how many candidates are ready (0 at this point)

Supported formats: PDF, DOCX, PNG, JPG, WEBP, TXT

docs.title: Step 3: Parse CVs
docs.text:
1. Click "Parse All (OCR)"
  2. The system automatically activates the OCR service (you may see the GPU badge change to "OCR active")
  3. Wait for parsing to complete — the status message shows progress
  4. All candidates now show yellow "Parsed" badges
  5. At this point, VICA has read the text from each CV and removed personal information (names, emails, phone numbers)

What if parsing fails? Check that the backend is running. For non-PDF text files, OCR is not needed.

docs.title: Step 4: Extract Skills
docs.text:
1. Click "Extract Skills (LLM)"
  2. The system automatically switches to the AI model (GPU badge changes to "LLM active")
  3. This step takes longer — the AI reads each CV individually and identifies skills with evidence
  4. When complete, candidates show green "Ready" badges
  5. The progress bar shows "N/N ready"
  6. The "Next" button is now enabled

What happens here: For each CV, the AI identifies every professional and technical skill, how proficient the candidate appears to be, and the exact CV text that supports each skill claim.

docs.title: Step 5: Review & Validate Job Skills
docs.text:
1. Click "Next" to go to the Description & Skills tab
  2. VICA's AI automatically extracts skills from your job description
  3. You'll see a list of skills with:
  4. Drag handles on the left — drag to reorder by importance (most important at top)
  5. Checkboxes — checked = Required (green border), unchecked = Preferred (gray border)
  6. Delete (X) button — remove skills that aren't relevant
  7. Review the list carefully:
  8. Are the most important skills at the top?
  9. Are the right skills marked as "Required" vs "Preferred"?
  10. Are there any irrelevant skills to remove?
  11. Click "Save Skills" — a "Saved" badge confirms

This step matters: The order and required/preferred classification directly affect how candidates are ranked. Skills at the top of the list carry more weight.

docs.title: Step 6: Run Match
docs.text:
1. Click "Next" to go to the Match & Reports tab
  2. Set the "Top candidates" number (how many candidates you want shortlisted)
  3. Click "Run Match"
  4. A spinner shows progress:
  5. First: "Ranking..." — the algorithm scores all candidates
  6. Then: "Analyzing candidate 1/N..." — the AI writes a detailed analysis for each shortlisted candidate
  7. When complete, you'll see a results table with:
  8. Rank — position in the shortlist
  9. Candidate — filename or ID
  10. Fitness score — how well they match (higher = better match)
  11. Report buttons — view detailed analysis

docs.title: Step 7: Read Reports
docs.text:
1. Click "View Report" on any candidate to see the detailed report inline
  2. Click the arrow icon to open the report in a new tab
  3. Each report contains:
  4. Recommendation badge — Strong Yes / Yes / Maybe / No
  5. Overall fit — 1-2 sentence summary
  6. Strengths and risks — bullet-point highlights
  7. Required skills table — each skill with match evidence and CV citations
  8. Preferred skills table — same format
  9. Transferable skills — relevant skills not in the job requirements
  10. Interview questions — targeted questions to ask this candidate

For details on how to interpret each report section, see Understanding Reports.

docs.title: Tips & FAQ
docs.text:
Q: What if I disagree with the ranking? Go back to Step 5 and adjust the skill order or required/preferred toggles. Rerun the match — results will change based on your adjustments.

Q: What if no skills are extracted from a CV? The CV text might be too short, in an unusual format, or the AI couldn't parse it. Check that parsing completed (yellow "Parsed" badge). If the CV is an image-only PDF, ensure OCR is working.

Q: Can I add more candidates after running a match? Yes. Upload and process new CVs, then rerun the match.

Q: What if the AI service is unavailable? VICA falls back to keyword-based skill matching (less accurate but still functional). The GPU status badge will indicate when services are not available.

Q: How long does it take? - Parsing: a few seconds per CV - Skill extraction: 15-60 seconds per CV (depends on length and GPU) - Matching + analysis: 30-90 seconds per shortlisted candidate

For detailed UI testing steps, see the Testing Guide.

docs.title: Understanding Candidate Reports
docs.text: After VICA ranks candidates, it generates a detailed report for each shortlisted candidate. This guide explains every section of the report and how to use it in your hiring process.

docs.title: Report Overview
docs.text:
The report compares one candidate against one job. It shows: - How well the candidate's skills match the job requirements - Evidence from the CV supporting each skill - Where the candidate is strong, and where there are gaps - Targeted interview questions to verify claims

What the report does NOT do: It does not assign a numerical "fit score out of 10". It does not make a hiring decision. It provides evidence and analysis to support your judgment.

docs.title: Recommendation Badge
docs.text:
At the top of the report, you'll see one of four badges:

Badge What it means What to do Strong Yes Excellent fit — strong evidence across required skills Move to interview, high priority Yes Good fit — meets most requirements with solid evidence Move to interview, standard priority Maybe Mixed signals — some gaps or unsubstantiated claims Review carefully, consider if gaps are dealbreakers No Not suitable — missing critical required skills Deprioritize unless circumstances change

docs.title: Overall Fit
docs.text: A 1-2 sentence assessment summarizing the candidate's fit. Read this first for a quick impression before diving into details.

docs.title: Strengths & Risks
docs.text:
Two bullet-point lists:

  • Strengths (green): 2-4 key things going for this candidate. These are the reasons to interview them.
  • Risks / Gaps (red): 1-3 concerns. These are areas to probe in the interview or reasons for caution.

How to use: Strengths validate the candidate. Risks become the basis for your interview questions — if VICA flags a gap, ask about it.

docs.title: Required Skills Table
docs.text:
A table with one row per required job skill. Each row shows:

Column What it means Skill name The required skill from your job description Status Check mark (has it) or cross (doesn't have it) Match level How well the candidate matches this specific skill Evidence Summary of relevant CV content

docs.title: Match Level Badges
docs.text: Badge Meaning Typical action Strong match (green) Clear direct experience with this skill No concerns for this skill Partial match (yellow) Some evidence but not deep, or related but not exact Ask about depth in interview Transferable (blue) Has a closely related skill that could transfer Ask about willingness/ability to transition No match (red) No evidence of this or related skills Major gap — assess if trainable or dealbreaker

docs.title: CV Citations
docs.text:
Under each skill assessment, you may see "CV Citations:" followed by exact quotes from the candidate's CV. These are: - Copied verbatim from the CV (not paraphrased) - Full sentences or bullet points (not fragments) - From multiple CV sections if the skill appears in different places

Why this matters: Citations let you verify the AI's assessment. If a skill is marked "strong_match", the citations should show real project experience. If citations seem thin, the match might be weaker than it appears.

docs.title: Preferred Skills Table
docs.text: Same format as the required skills table, but for "nice to have" skills. These skills carry less weight in the ranking but can differentiate between similar candidates.

docs.title: Transferable Skills
docs.text:
Cards showing skills the candidate has that were not in your job requirements but are relevant. Each card shows:

  • Skill name: The candidate's skill
  • Relevant to: Which job skill(s) it could complement or substitute
  • Reasoning: Why this skill is transferable or valuable

How to use: Transferable skills can be a tiebreaker. A candidate with "PostgreSQL" experience when you asked for "SQL" has transferable knowledge. A candidate with "project management" when you didn't ask for it might bring unexpected value.

docs.title: Interview Questions
docs.text: 4-8 targeted questions designed to help you interview this specific candidate. Questions come in two categories:

docs.title: Verification Questions (blue badge)
docs.text:
These probe skills the candidate claimed but with little supporting evidence. They help you determine whether the candidate really has these skills.

Example: "You list Kubernetes in your skills section but your CV doesn't describe any container orchestration projects. Can you describe a specific production deployment you managed with Kubernetes?"

When to use: Always ask verification questions — they catch inflated CVs.

docs.title: Technical Depth Questions (orange badge)
docs.text:
These test the depth of the candidate's strongest skills. They go beyond what the CV describes to see if the candidate has real expertise.

Example: "You architected the migration from monolith to microservices at CompanyX. How did you handle distributed transactions across services, and what trade-offs did you make?"

When to use: Use these to differentiate between candidates who look similar on paper.

docs.title: Priority Badges
docs.text:
Each question has a priority:

Priority Meaning High Critical to ask — addresses a key uncertainty Medium Important — helps complete the picture Low Nice to have — for deeper exploration if time allows

docs.title: Additional Skills
docs.text: A list of skills the candidate has that were not part of your job requirements or transferable skills. Shows skill name and proficiency level. Useful for understanding the candidate's broader profile.

docs.title: Using Reports in Your Process
docs.text:
1. First pass: Read the recommendation badge and overall fit for each candidate
  2. Compare: Look at required skills tables side by side — who has more green badges?
  3. Prepare interviews: Use the interview questions directly or as inspiration
  4. Verify: Check CV citations for skills you care most about
  5. Decide: Use strengths/risks to justify your shortlist decisions

Remember: VICA assists your judgment. The final decision is always yours.

docs.title: What is Validation?
docs.text:
Validation lets you measure how well VICA's ranking matches real hiring outcomes. You can answer questions like: - "If we had used VICA for our last hiring round, would it have recommended the same candidates we interviewed?" - "Does VICA's ranking agree with my expert judgment?"

This helps you build confidence in the system and tune it over time.

docs.title: Historical Validation
docs.text:
Use past hiring data where you already know who was interviewed and hired. Upload the CVs from a completed hiring round, let VICA rank them, then compare its ranking to your actual decisions.

Best for: Backtesting, building stakeholder confidence, measuring accuracy over time.

docs.title: Supervised Validation
docs.text:
Run a side-by-side comparison: you independently pick your top candidates, then VICA ranks them separately. Compare the two shortlists.

Best for: Current hiring rounds, calibrating VICA against your judgment, identifying blind spots.

docs.title: 1. Navigate to Validation
docs.text: From the left sidebar, click Validation Runs (or use the New Validation Run quick action). You'll see a list of any existing validation runs with search and filter controls to quickly find specific runs.

docs.title: 2. Create a New Run
docs.text:
1. Click "New Validation Run"
  2. Enter a title (e.g., "Q1 2026 Backend Hiring" or "Senior Engineer - March")
  3. Choose the type:
  4. Historical — you know who was interviewed/hired
  5. Supervised — you'll make independent picks
  6. Click Create

docs.title: 3. Upload Candidates
docs.text:
1. In the validation run detail view, upload CVs (same as the regular hiring loop)
  2. Click "Parse All" to extract text from CVs
  3. Click "Extract Skills" to identify skills from each CV
  4. Wait for all candidates to show green "Ready" badges

docs.title: 4. Set the Job Description
docs.text:
1. Paste or upload the job description text
  2. Click "Save JD"
  3. The system extracts skills from the JD
  4. Review and validate the skills (reorder, toggle required/preferred, delete irrelevant ones)
  5. Click "Save Skills"

docs.title: 5a. Historical: Mark Past Outcomes
docs.text:
If you chose Historical mode:

  1. For each candidate, set their actual outcome using the status selector:
  2. Interviewed — was interviewed (whether or not they were hired)
  3. Hired — was ultimately hired
  4. Rejected — was not selected for interview
  5. These outcomes are your ground truth — what actually happened

Tip: You don't need to mark every candidate. The ones you mark as "interviewed" or "hired" are the positive set. Everyone else is treated as negative.

docs.title: 5b. Supervised: Make Your Picks
docs.text:
If you chose Supervised mode:

  1. Review the candidates (you can look at CVs, but not at VICA's ranking — it hasn't run yet)
  2. Select the candidates you would shortlist based on your expert judgment
  3. Save your picks

Important: Make your picks BEFORE running the match. The comparison is only meaningful if your picks are independent of VICA's ranking.

docs.title: 6. Run the Match
docs.text:
1. Click "Run Match"
  2. VICA ranks ALL candidates using SPEA2
  3. Then analyzes the top candidates with the AI
  4. Progress is streamed — you'll see "Ranking..." then "Analyzing candidate 1/N..."

docs.title: 7. Compute Statistics
docs.text:
1. After the match completes, click "Compute Stats"
  2. VICA compares its top-K ranking against your ground truth and calculates:

Key metrics explained in plain language:

Metric What it answers Precision "Of the candidates VICA picked, how many did you also pick?" Recall "Of the candidates you picked, how many did VICA find?" F1 Score Combined measure of precision and recall (higher = better agreement) Accuracy@K "What fraction of VICA's top-K are actual positives?"

Example: If you interviewed 5 candidates and VICA's top 5 includes 3 of them: - Precision = ⅗ = 60% ("3 of VICA's 5 picks were correct") - Recall = ⅗ = 60% ("VICA found 3 of the 5 you interviewed") - F1 = 60%

docs.title: 8. View the Stats Report
docs.text: Click "View Report" to see a detailed HTML report including: - Core metrics (Precision, Recall, F1, Accuracy@K) - Rank analysis: Where did your actual picks land in VICA's full ranking? (average rank, best rank, worst rank) - Skill coverage: How many candidates have each skill - Fitness distribution: Are ground-truth candidates scoring higher than others? - True/False Positives: Which candidates VICA got right vs wrong

docs.title: 9. Aggregate Report
docs.text: If you've run multiple validation rounds, click "Aggregate Report" to see: - Average precision, recall, F1 across all runs - Standard deviation (consistency measure) - Total candidates evaluated and total ground truth size

docs.title: What "good" metrics look like
docs.text: Metric Good Okay Needs improvement Precision > 70% 50-70% < 50% Recall > 70% 50-70% < 50% F1 > 70% 50-70% < 50%

docs.title: Common situations
docs.text:
High precision, low recall: VICA is conservative — the candidates it picks are good, but it's missing some that you would have picked. Consider increasing the top-K size.

Low precision, high recall: VICA is recommending too broadly — it finds all your candidates but also includes many you wouldn't pick. The skill ordering or required/preferred classification may need tuning.

Low precision AND low recall: The skill list may not match what you're actually evaluating. Review the job skills — are the right skills marked as required? Is the ordering correct?

docs.title: What to adjust
docs.text:
• Skill order: Move the most important skills to the top
  • Required vs preferred: Make sure critical skills are "required"
  • Skill list: Add missing skills or remove irrelevant ones
  • Top-K size: Adjust how many candidates VICA shortlists

After adjusting, run a new validation to see if metrics improve.

For the mathematical definitions of all metrics, see Validation Metrics.
